proposals.title: Influence of Advanced Combustion and Gasification Technologies on the Signature of Organic Molecular Markers in Emissions and Residues
proposals.motivation: Organic molecular markers, such as polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and hopanes, are widely used as tracers for source apportionment of atmospheric particulates and environmental forensics, assuming their signatures are conserved across combustion processes. However, the rapid deployment of advanced combustion technologies (e.g., oxy-fuel, chemical looping, high-efficiency gasification) for cleaner energy and waste treatment may fundamentally alter thermal degradation pathways, challenging the validity of these established tracers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the yields, isomer distributions, and diagnostic ratios of key organic marker compounds (e.g., PAHs, alkanes, resin-derived biomarkers) are significantly dependent on combustion technology parameters (temperature, oxygen availability, residence time, catalysis), rendering some traditional source signatures obsolete.
proposals.method: Controlled laboratory-scale combustion experiments will be performed on standardized fuels (including lignite and synthetic waste) using a drop-tube furnace configured for different technologies (conventional, oxy-fuel, gasification). Solid residues (ash, char) and captured emissions will be analyzed via GC-MS and Py-GC-MS for a comprehensive suite of organic markers. Multivariate statistical approaches (Principal Component Analysis, hierarchical clustering) will correlate specific marker concentrations and ratios with operational parameters to identify technology-dependent chemical fingerprints.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a critical, quantitative framework for updating environmental forensics and source apportionment models in the era of advanced thermal conversion. It will identify new, robust tracer combinations resistant to technological change and flag vulnerable existing markers, directly informing air quality and sediment core studies.
proposals.novelty: While previous work identifies markers for broad source categories (e.g., coal vs. biomass), the systematic investigation of how cutting-edge combustion engineering alters these fundamental geochemical signatures is novel, bridging process engineering with environmental organic geochemistry.

proposals.title: Apportioning Ship and Wood Combustion Aerosols in Coastal and Urban Atmospheres Using Advanced Organic Tracer-Based Receptor Modeling
proposals.motivation: Ship emissions and residential wood combustion are significant, yet poorly quantified, sources of particulate matter (PM) and toxic organic compounds in coastal and urban areas. Pre-2026 air quality management relies heavily on receptor models like Positive Matrix Factorization (PMF) to apportion urban PM sources, but these techniques are rarely applied with the high-resolution organic molecular tracers needed to distinguish specific combustion types. This limits effective policy interventions for these key emission sectors.
proposals.hypothesis: Advanced multivariate receptor modeling techniques, when applied to detailed organic molecular marker datasets (e.g., hopanes, steranes, resin acids, methoxyphenols, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons), can robustly quantify and separate the contributions of ship (heavy fuel oil) and wood (biomass burning) combustion to ambient aerosols in mixed-source environments.
proposals.method: We will collect PM samples from strategic coastal and urban sites. Samples will be analyzed via GC-MS for a comprehensive suite of organic tracers, including those for fossil combustion (e.g., hopanes from lubricating oils), ship emissions (e.g., specific PAH ratios, vanadium), and biomass burning (e.g., levoglucosan, specific methoxyphenols, resin acids). These concentration data will be input into the US EPA PMF 5.0 model. We will employ rigorous uncertainty estimation, factor profiling guided by source-specific tracers, and rotational control to resolve chemically distinct source factors. The model will be validated using synthetic datasets and collocated measurements of inorganic tracers (e.g., metals).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, tracer-based methodological framework for quantitatively apportioning ship and wood combustion aerosols. It will yield specific emission profiles and contribution estimates for these sources in case study regions, directly informing air quality management strategies. The research will bridge the gap between detailed organic geochemistry and applied atmospheric source apportionment.
proposals.novelty: While receptor modeling is standard for urban PM, its targeted application using a refined suite of geochemical tracers to explicitly separate and quantify ship and wood combustion—two sources often conflated in traditional models—is novel. This adapts cutting-edge air quality management tools to address a specific, complex problem in combustion aerosol science.

proposals.title: Characterization of Organic Compound-Associated Nanoparticle Emissions from Modern Biomass Combustion Appliances: A Molecular Tracer and Toxic Element Approach
proposals.motivation: Modern masonry heaters and pellet boilers are promoted as efficient, low-emission alternatives for residential heating. However, pre-2026 research indicates that even efficient combustion can generate significant ultrafine particle (UFP) emissions, which pose serious health risks due to their deep lung penetration and high surface area for toxic compound adsorption. The specific organic molecular composition and associated toxic trace elements (e.g., Hg, As) bound to these nanoparticles from modern appliances remain poorly characterized, creating a critical knowledge gap for accurate health impact assessment and emission regulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that nanoparticle emissions from modern, high-efficiency masonry heaters and pellet boilers contain distinct suites of organic molecular tracers (e.g., specific polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, hopanes, steranes) and carry significant concentrations of toxic elements, with compositional signatures that vary systematically with appliance type, fuel quality, and combustion phase.
proposals.method: Emission sampling will be conducted in a controlled laboratory setting using a dilution tunnel and a Nano-MOUDI impactor coupled with quartz fiber filters to collect size-resolved particles, including the sub-100 nm fraction. Organic compounds will be extracted, fractionated, and analyzed via Gas Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry (GC-MS) to identify and quantify specific molecular tracers (e.g., PAHs, alkanes, biomarkers). Concurrently, total particulate matter and size fractions will be analyzed for trace elements (Hg, As, Pb, Se) using Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometry (ICP-MS). Source apportionment will employ multivariate statistical approaches (Principal Component Analysis, Positive Matrix Factorization) to link emission profiles to combustion conditions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed molecular and elemental fingerprint of nanoparticle emissions from modern biomass heating technologies. It will establish a robust dataset of organic and inorganic tracers specific to these sources, directly contributing to more accurate source apportionment in atmospheric studies. The findings will inform regulatory frameworks and technology design by identifying key emission components linked to operational variables.
proposals.novelty: While Simoneit's prior work has applied organic geochemistry to identify pollution sources like coal waste and marine sediments, applying this sophisticated tracer methodology to characterize nanoparticles from specific, modern combustion appliances is novel. The integration of detailed organic molecular marker analysis with toxic element quantification for UFPs in this context is unprecedented.

proposals.title: Inverse Modeling of Ship Fuel Types Using Ambient Organic Marker Concentrations and Multivariate Statistical Analysis
proposals.motivation: Ship emissions are a major source of atmospheric pollutants and greenhouse gases, but compliance monitoring and source apportionment for marine fuel types (e.g., heavy fuel oil, marine diesel oil, liquefied natural gas) remains challenging. Current methods often rely on direct stack sampling or fuel sulfur content, lacking a robust, ambient-based forensic tool for identifying fuel types from environmental samples. This gap hinders effective enforcement of emission control areas and understanding of shipping's impact on coastal air quality.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific suites of organic molecular markers (e.g., hopanes, steranes, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, alkanes) and their diagnostic ratios, preserved in ambient particulate matter, are unique to different commercial marine fuel types and combustion conditions, enabling their discrimination through multivariate statistical modeling.
proposals.method: We will collect ambient PM2.5 samples from coastal and port sites using high-volume air samplers. Organic compounds will be extracted, fractionated, and analyzed via GC-MS for a comprehensive suite of known petroleum and combustion markers. A reference library of source profiles will be constructed by analyzing direct emissions from ships using known fuels. The inverse problem will be solved using multivariate statistical approaches, including principal component analysis (PCA) for dimensionality reduction and linear discriminant analysis (LDA) or non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) to classify ambient samples into fuel type categories based on their marker fingerprints.
proposals.contribution: This work will develop a novel, ambient air-based methodology for ship fuel type identification, contributing a new tool for regulatory compliance and emission inventory validation. It will advance the field of organic geochemistry applied to atmospheric forensics by establishing diagnostic marker suites for modern marine fuels, extending principles used in oil-source correlation to contemporary pollution studies.
proposals.novelty: While source apportionment of ship emissions is established, the explicit inverse modeling to diagnose the specific fuel type burned from ambient markers, rather than just quantifying the ship contribution, is a novel application. This approach integrates detailed organic geochemical fingerprinting, common in petroleum systems analysis, with multivariate statistical methods used in environmental forensics to solve a new problem in maritime emission monitoring.

proposals.title: Quantifying Causal Drivers of Seasonal Volatile Organic Compound Speciation in an Urban-Coastal Environment Using High-Resolution Monitoring and Convergent Cross Mapping
proposals.motivation: Seasonal variations in atmospheric VOC composition significantly impact air quality and secondary organic aerosol formation, yet existing studies often report correlations without establishing causality. Disentangling the mechanistic contributions of biogenic emissions, anthropogenic sources, and meteorological forcing is critical for developing accurate predictive models and effective emission control strategies, especially in complex environments influenced by both urban and marine air masses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the seasonal dynamics of specific VOC molecular markers (e.g., isoprene, monoterpenes, benzene, toluene) are causally driven by distinct, quantifiable mechanisms: temperature-dependent biogenic emissions, cold-start vehicle emissions modulated by boundary layer height, and photochemical aging rates controlled by solar radiation.
proposals.method: We will implement a one-year, continuous high-resolution monitoring campaign using a Proton-Transfer-Reaction Time-of-Flight Mass Spectrometer (PTR-TOF-MS) co-located with meteorological sensors, a traffic counter, and boundary layer height data from a co-located ceilometer. The causal inference will be performed using the Convergent Cross Mapping (CCM) algorithm, a state-of-the-art (pre-2026) method for detecting causality in nonlinear dynamical systems from time-series data. This will be complemented by multivariate statistical analyses (Positive Matrix Factorization) to apportion source contributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, causal model for VOC seasonality, moving beyond descriptive statistics. It will deliver quantified attribution fractions for key drivers, offering a novel framework for validating and improving chemical transport models. The high-resolution dataset will serve as a benchmark for understanding VOC chemistry in mixed urban-biogenic environments.
proposals.novelty: The application of CCM for causal attribution in atmospheric VOC dynamics is novel, as prior work relies on correlation or lag-regression. Integrating this with a comprehensive, co-located multi-parameter dataset specifically designed for causal testing represents a significant methodological advance over traditional monitoring studies.

proposals.title: Atmospheric Metabolism of Liquefied Petroleum Gas: Quantifying Secondary Ozone Formation Potential via Detailed Chemical Mechanism Modeling
proposals.motivation: Liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) is promoted as a clean alternative fuel, but its high Ozone Formation Potential (OFP) indicates a significant, under-quantified role in urban photochemical smog. Current regulatory frameworks focus on primary emissions, potentially overlooking the complex secondary chemistry of LPG's atmospheric oxidation products. This gap is critical for effective air quality management in cities where LPG use is widespread.
proposals.hypothesis: The atmospheric oxidation of LPG components (primarily propane and butanes) acts as a 'prodrug' mechanism, generating reactive carbonyl intermediates and peroxy radicals that disproportionately contribute to ozone formation compared to its primary emission profile.
proposals.method: We will apply the Master Chemical Mechanism (MCM v3.3.1) to map the complete gas-phase atmospheric oxidation pathways for propane, n-butane, and i-butane under varied NOx and VOC regimes. Model outputs will be used to quantify production rates and yields of key ozone-forming intermediates (e.g., acetaldehyde, formaldehyde, peroxyacetyl nitrate). These mechanistic results will be integrated with real-world urban emission inventories (e.g., from traffic and residential sectors) using a box model to calculate scenario-specific OFP contributions, treating LPG as a tracer for its secondary pollutant suite.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles, mechanistic assessment of LPG's full atmospheric 'chemical lifecycle' and its role as an indirect pollution source. It will deliver a refined, pathway-based OFP metric for LPG, moving beyond empirical reactivity scales. The findings will inform more accurate air quality models and policies regarding the net environmental impact of 'clean' fuel alternatives.
proposals.novelty: The study innovatively applies a pharmacological prodrug metabolism analogy to atmospheric chemistry, shifting focus from primary emissions to secondary reaction networks. It adapts Dr. Simoneit's expertise in using specific organic compounds as tracers for complex processes (e.g., oil cracking, PAH sourcing) to the dynamic, gas-phase photochemical system of urban air pollution.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Atmospheric Impact of LPG: A Bayesian Probabilistic Framework for Apportioning Secondary Ozone Formation Potential
proposals.motivation: Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG) is promoted as a cleaner-burning alternative fuel, but its volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions, particularly propane and butane, are potent ozone precursors. Traditional source apportionment and ozone formation potential (OFP) calculations provide point estimates, failing to capture the significant uncertainties in emission profiles, atmospheric chemistry, and source contributions. This gap hinders robust policy evaluation of fuel-switching strategies aimed at improving urban air quality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that LPG is a dominant and highly uncertain contributor to secondary ozone formation in urban areas, and that a probabilistic assessment will reveal a broader, riskier impact distribution than deterministic methods suggest, potentially altering the net benefit calculus of LPG adoption policies.
proposals.method: We will apply Bayesian Positive Matrix Factorization (BPMF) to high-time-resolution ambient VOC measurements from urban monitoring sites to probabilistically apportion sources, including LPG. The posterior distributions of LPG-specific VOC concentrations will be propagated through the Detailed Hydrocarbon Protocol and the SAPRC-07 chemical mechanism to calculate probability distributions of OFP. Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling will be used to quantify and trace uncertainties from source contribution through to the final OFP metric, comparing the resulting distributions against those from other sources like vehicle exhaust and biogenic emissions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first risk-aware, probabilistic framework for evaluating the secondary pollution impact of LPG, moving beyond deterministic 'best estimates'. It will deliver a quantitative tool for policymakers to assess the likelihood that fuel-switching to LPG achieves ozone reduction targets, explicitly incorporating uncertainty into environmental decision-making.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of Bayesian source apportionment with explicit chemical mechanism modeling to propagate full uncertainty distributions, rather than single values, for OFP. This creates a non-obvious, probabilistic counterpoint to the standard 'clean fuel' narrative for LPG.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Secondary Ozone Formation Potential of Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG) Emissions: A Molecular Tracer and Reactivity-Weighted Approach
proposals.motivation: Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG) is widely promoted as a cleaner alternative to coal and gasoline due to lower primary emissions of particulates and NOx. However, its high volatility and specific hydrocarbon composition (e.g., propane, butanes) may act as potent precursors for secondary photochemical ozone formation. Pre-2026 air quality management often focuses on primary pollutants, potentially underestimating LPG's role in urban and regional ozone episodes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that atmospheric emissions from LPG combustion and fugitive releases contribute disproportionately to ozone formation potential (OFP) relative to their primary emission mass, due to the high photochemical reactivity of key tracer compounds.
proposals.method: The study will employ source-specific sampling (urban, industrial, vehicular) to collect atmospheric particulate and vapor-phase samples. Organic compounds will be extracted and analyzed using GC-MS. We will quantify known LPG tracers (e.g., specific light alkanes, cyclic hydrocarbons) and apply pre-2026 Maximum Incremental Reactivity (MIR) scales to calculate their OFP. Multivariate statistical analyses (PCA, PMF) will be used to apportion LPG's contribution to total OFP compared to other VOC sources (e.g., vehicle exhaust, biomass burning) in receptor environments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, reactivity-based assessment of LPG's air quality impact, moving beyond primary emissions. It will deliver a refined source profile for LPG emissions weighted by ozone-forming potential. The findings will inform policymakers and emission inventory models on the need to regulate LPG's reactive organic compounds to effectively mitigate secondary ozone pollution.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard 'clean fuel' narrative by systematically evaluating LPG through the lens of secondary pollution formation, integrating Dr. Simoneit's expertise in molecular tracer analysis and multivariate source apportionment specifically for ozone precursor studies.

proposals.title: Reconstructing the Evolution of Urban Emission Signatures: A Dynamic Source Apportionment Framework for Receptor Models
proposals.motivation: Receptor models like Positive Matrix Factorization (PMF) are central to urban air quality management but rely on static source profiles. This is a critical flaw, as real-world emission sources, especially vehicular fleets, undergo rapid technological evolution (e.g., diesel phase-outs, GDI proliferation, after-treatment systems). Using fixed profiles for multi-year datasets can lead to significant misattribution of pollution sources, undermining the effectiveness of control strategies. This problem mirrors the challenge in paleoclimatology, where the geochemical fingerprint of dust sources in ice cores changes over geological time due to climate forcing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that constructing a time-resolved library of vehicle emission profiles, informed by fleet composition and technology penetration data, and integrating it into receptor modeling will significantly improve the accuracy and temporal consistency of source apportionment results for urban aerosols and volatile organic compounds (VOCs).
proposals.method: The research will first compile a high-resolution, time-varying emission profile library. This will synthesize historical data on fleet turnover, fuel standards, and technology adoption (e.g., EURO norms) with published source testing studies (e.g., VOC/PM ratios, NOx speciation, organic marker compounds like hopanes and steranes). We will then develop a modified PMF framework where source profiles are not fixed but are allowed to 'drift' within constrained bounds defined by the temporal library, using a rolling-window or time-sliced PMF approach. The model will be validated against long-term monitoring data from an urban site, using known regulatory events (e.g., a diesel ban) as temporal markers for expected shifts in source contributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first operational framework for dynamic chemical mass balance in atmospheric science, moving beyond the static profile paradigm. It will deliver a more accurate historical reconstruction of source contributions, directly informing the evaluation of past air quality policies. The methodology will be a critical tool for future projections, allowing models to account for the evolving chemical nature of anthropogenic emissions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit translation of the paleoclimatology concept of evolving source signatures—applied to dust in ice cores—to the much faster timescales of anthropogenic technological change, creating a dynamic receptor modeling architecture. This directly challenges a foundational assumption in source apportionment that has persisted since its inception.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Toxicological Profiling of Photochemically Aged Liquefied Petroleum Gas: Linking Source-Specific Organic Tracers to Cellular Response
proposals.motivation: Liquefied petroleum gas (LPG), a major global fuel, undergoes atmospheric oxidation to form secondary organic aerosols (SOA) and other products with poorly characterized health impacts. Pre-2026 studies on complex emissions, like coal waste or marine sediments, rely on detailed organic tracer analysis but lack high-throughput methods to systematically link specific fuel compositions to toxicological outcomes. This gap hinders risk assessment for variable real-world LPG sources.
proposals.hypothesis: The oxidative aging of LPG from different geological and refining sources will produce distinct mixtures of oxidized organic compounds, leading to quantifiable and source-dependent differences in cellular toxicity, correlating with specific tracer compounds beyond bulk propane/butane.
proposals.method: We will integrate a liquid-handling robotic system (e.g., Hamilton STAR) with a multi-chamber photochemical reactor. The robot will prepare fuel mixtures using commercial LPG samples and synthetic blends spiked with source-specific tracers (e.g., light hydrocarbons, sulfur compounds). These will be injected into parallel Teflon chambers for controlled OH-radical aging. The resulting oxidized mixtures will be directly transferred in a high-throughput format to 96-well plates containing human lung cell lines (e.g., A549) for cytotoxicity and oxidative stress assays (e.g., MTT, ROS). Analytical chemistry (GC-MS, HPLC) will quantify tracer evolution and SOA composition.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first high-throughput pipeline connecting LPG source chemistry to biological effect, generating a dataset mapping specific organic tracer concentrations to toxicological endpoints. It will advance the field beyond descriptive emission studies by providing a mechanistic, rapid-screening tool for fuel and emission regulation.
proposals.novelty: The novel fusion of automated environmental chamber technology with robotic toxicological screening creates a closed-loop system for rapid, parallel assessment of complex fuel oxidation, a significant departure from traditional low-throughput, discrete chamber-to-assay studies.

proposals.title: Toxicity and Health Risk Assessment of Volatile Organic Compound Mixtures Dominated by Liquefied Petroleum Gas Tracers in Urban and Industrial Atmospheres
proposals.motivation: Liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) is a major urban and domestic fuel, with its use increasing globally. While its combustion is considered cleaner than other fossil fuels, fugitive emissions and incomplete combustion release a distinct VOC profile dominated by propane and butanes, whose combined toxicity and health risks in complex ambient mixtures are poorly characterized. This gap is critical for assessing the true public health impact of the ongoing energy transition towards LPG.
proposals.hypothesis: The VOC mixture characteristic of LPG emissions, while dominated by low-toxicity alkanes, presents a significant additive or synergistic health risk due to co-emitted, more toxic species and the potential for secondary atmospheric reactions forming hazardous oxidation products.
proposals.method: We will collect ambient air samples from LPG-dominated source zones (e.g., distribution hubs, residential areas) and urban background sites using thermal desorption tubes. VOC analysis will be performed via GC-MS, with a focus on quantifying LPG tracers (C2-C5 alkanes, alkenes) and co-emitted hazardous air pollutants (e.g., benzene, formaldehyde). Health risk will be evaluated using the US EPA's inhalation risk assessment framework, calculating hazard indices and cancer risks for individual and combined VOCs. Multivariate statistical analysis (Principal Component Analysis and Positive Matrix Factorization) will be used to apportion the LPG-specific source contribution and its associated toxic burden.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative health risk assessment specifically tied to the atmospheric VOC fingerprint of LPG. It will establish a source-specific risk profile, informing regulatory policies on fugitive emissions and fuel switching strategies. The work will also advance methods for linking complex organic compound mixtures from specific sources to human health endpoints.
proposals.novelty: Previous health assessments of VOCs often treat them as a generic class or focus on known toxics like benzene; this work uniquely targets the health implications of a specific, increasingly prevalent fuel-source signature, integrating source apportionment with toxicological risk modeling for its composite mixture.

proposals.title: Observationally Constrained Photochemistry: Drone-Based Mass Spectrometry of LPG Tracers to Validate and Refine Urban Ozone Formation Models
proposals.motivation: Liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) is a significant urban volatile organic compound (VOC) source, but its contribution to ozone formation remains poorly constrained due to reliance on static emission inventories and coarse monitoring networks. Pre-2026 studies highlight the need for source-specific, dynamic validation of photochemical mechanisms within actual urban plumes to improve predictive air quality models. This gap limits the efficacy of targeted mitigation strategies for secondary pollutants like ozone.
proposals.hypothesis: Real-time, high-resolution measurements of LPG-specific tracers (e.g., n-butane, i-butane, propane) and ozone precursors within urban plumes will reveal dynamic emission factors and chemical processing rates that differ significantly from current model parameterizations, leading to systematic errors in simulated ozone production.
proposals.method: We will deploy rotary-wing UAVs equipped with miniaturized, custom-configured proton-transfer-reaction time-of-flight mass spectrometers (PTR-TOF-MS) to perform targeted grid and vertical profile sampling downwind of identified LPG source areas (e.g., distribution terminals, industrial kitchens). Flight paths will capture fresh and aged plume gradients. Measured concentration ratios and decay rates of LPG tracers relative to co-emitted species (e.g., NOx, CO) will be assimilated into a modified version of the Weather Research and Forecasting model coupled with Chemistry (WRF-Chem), using an ensemble Kalman filter to iteratively optimize the model's LPG emission factors and oxidation rate constants.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first observationally derived, spatially explicit dynamic emission factors for urban LPG sources. It will directly test and refine the representation of LPG chemistry in a leading photochemical model, moving beyond inventory-based assumptions. The result will be a fundamentally improved, mechanistic link between LPG emissions and ozone formation, providing a validated framework for more accurate pollution forecasting and source attribution.
proposals.novelty: The integration of drone-based, near-source PTR-TOF-MS measurements with ensemble-based chemical data assimilation for urban plume model validation is a novel, high-resolution approach. It moves beyond static tracer studies to dynamically constrain the evolving photochemistry of a specific VOC source mix in real atmospheric plumes.

proposals.title: Constraining Urban Ozone Production: Refining Photochemical Model Performance with Field-Measured LPG Tracer Abundances
proposals.motivation: Urban ozone remains a persistent air quality and public health challenge globally. Current photochemical models, which inform regulatory policy, rely on emission inventories with significant uncertainties, particularly for volatile organic compound (VOC) sources like liquefied petroleum gas (LPG). Incorporating direct, field-based measurements of source-specific tracers is critical for improving model accuracy and the effectiveness of ozone mitigation strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating measured atmospheric abundances of LPG-specific molecular tracers (e.g., specific n-alkanes, iso-alkanes) as constraints in a 3D photochemical model will significantly reduce uncertainty in simulated urban ozone concentrations and improve the apportionment of its precursors.
proposals.method: We will collect ambient air samples from a representative urban area and analyze them via thermal desorption-gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (TD-GC-MS) to quantify LPG tracer compounds. These measured concentrations will be used to derive top-down emission factors and scaling factors for the LPG source sector in a high-resolution emission inventory. This updated inventory will drive simulations using the Community Multiscale Air Quality (CMAQ) modeling system (v5.3 or similar). Model performance, evaluated against independent ozone monitoring data, will be compared between runs using default and tracer-constrained LPG emissions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, observationally constrained assessment of LPG's role in urban ozone photochemistry. It will deliver a methodological framework for using organic tracer data to validate and refine emission inventories for photochemical modeling. The results will offer actionable insights for air quality managers seeking to target the most impactful VOC sources.
proposals.novelty: While Simoneit's prior work used tracers for source apportionment in sediments, this proposal innovatively applies analogous organic geochemical tracer techniques to directly constrain a dynamic, 3D atmospheric photochemical model, moving from retrospective source identification to predictive simulation refinement.

proposals.title: Apportioning Volatile Organic Compound Sources in Urban-Industrial Atmospheres Using Chemical Mass Balance Modeling with High-Resolution Gas Chromatographic Fingerprints
proposals.motivation: Urban and industrial atmospheres contain complex mixtures of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) from diverse sources, including traffic, industrial emissions, and coal-related activities, which impact air quality and human health. Pre-2026 source apportionment studies often rely on limited tracer compounds or bulk parameters, lacking the resolution to distinguish between closely related emission sources, such as coal combustion, coal waste thermal alteration, and petroleum volatilization. A more detailed molecular-level approach is needed to accurately quantify contributions from these significant anthropogenic sources.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying Chemical Mass Balance (CMB) receptor modeling to highly detailed, source-specific VOC chromatographic fingerprints will significantly improve the resolution and accuracy of source apportionment in mixed urban-industrial environments, enabling the discrimination of contributions from thermally altered coal wastes, vehicle emissions, and petroleum-related sources.
proposals.method: The method involves collecting ambient air samples from a target urban-industrial area and potential source materials (e.g., lignite, coal waste, vehicle exhaust, resins). VOCs will be analyzed using high-resolution gas chromatography with mass spectrometry (GC-MS) to generate detailed molecular fingerprints. These source and receptor fingerprints will be used as input for the US EPA CMB 8.2 model, applying a least-squares solution to quantify source contributions. The model's performance will be validated using synthetic mixtures and by comparing apportioned polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) profiles with those from the researcher's previous sediment studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a refined, molecular-level framework for VOC source apportionment, directly applicable to air quality management. It will generate a novel library of chromatographic fingerprints for under-characterized sources like thermally affected coal wastes. The results will bridge the researcher's expertise in geological organic tracers with contemporary atmospheric chemistry, offering new insights into the atmospheric legacy of fossil fuel use and waste.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of detailed, high-resolution chromatographic fingerprints—akin to those used in oil-source correlation studies—into the established CMB framework for atmospheric VOCs, specifically targeting the discrimination of complex industrial and fossil fuel-derived sources that have overlapping chemical profiles.

proposals.title: Compound-Specific Isotopic Fingerprinting of Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs) to Discriminate Urban Emission Sources: LPG, Gasoline, and Diesel Combustion
proposals.motivation: Urban air quality is significantly impacted by volatile organic compounds (VOCs) from mobile and fuel sources, which act as ozone and secondary aerosol precursors. Pre-2026 source apportionment often relies on concentration ratios or molecular tracers, which can be ambiguous due to atmospheric mixing and degradation. A more robust method is needed to accurately partition contributions from key fossil fuel sources like LPG, gasoline, and diesel combustion for effective air quality management.
proposals.hypothesis: The compound-specific stable carbon (δ13C) and hydrogen (δ2H) isotope signatures of key VOCs (e.g., benzene, toluene, xylenes, n-alkanes) are distinct and conserved enough to serve as reliable fingerprints, allowing for quantitative discrimination of emissions from LPG evaporation/combustion, gasoline combustion, and diesel combustion in complex urban atmospheres.
proposals.method: The research will employ gas chromatography-isotope ratio mass spectrometry (GC-IRMS) for high-precision δ13C and δ2H analysis of target VOCs. Source samples will be collected directly: headspace of commercial LPG, exhaust from controlled gasoline and diesel engine tests. Ambient air samples will be collected using adsorbent tubes (e.g., Tenax TA) in urban areas. A multivariate isotopic mixing model, based on Bayesian inference (e.g., using the MixSIAR framework pre-2026), will be applied to quantify source contributions to the ambient VOC pool, using the measured source end-member isotopic ranges as priors.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, isotopic-based reference dataset for key urban VOC sources, moving beyond traditional tracer methods. It will provide a more definitive tool for atmospheric chemists and modelers to apportion VOC sources, reducing uncertainty in emission inventories. The methodology can be applied to other regions and integrated with receptor modeling to validate and refine emission control strategies.
proposals.novelty: While CSIA is established for PAHs and higher hydrocarbons in sediments (as in Simoneit's prior work), its systematic application to discriminate between specific, co-located combustion-derived VOC sources in the atmosphere using a dual-isotope (C & H) approach represents a significant and non-routine advancement in urban air pollution forensics.

proposals.title: Molecular Tracers Meet Metabarcoding: Identifying Plant-Derived Fuel Sources in Ambient Aerosols via Environmental DNA
proposals.motivation: Identifying specific plant sources of organic aerosols and particulate matter is critical for accurate source apportionment in air quality studies and for understanding the biogeochemical cycling of modern carbon. Traditional organic geochemical tracers (e.g., specific lipids, lignin phenols) provide broad classifications but can lack the taxonomic resolution needed to distinguish between, for example, different types of biomass burning or biogenic volatile organic compound (BVOC) emissions from specific local flora. This limits our ability to model and manage air pollution from biogenic and anthropogenic-biogenic sources.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that plant-derived environmental DNA (eDNA) extracted from ambient aerosol filters can be successfully sequenced using metabarcoding techniques to provide species-level or genus-level identification of the vascular plant sources contributing to the organic aerosol fraction, complementing and refining data from traditional molecular tracer analyses.
proposals.method: We will collect ambient PM2.5 and PM10 samples on quartz fiber filters from contrasting environments (e.g., urban, rural-forested). DNA will be extracted using optimized protocols for low-biomass aerosol samples. We will perform PCR amplification targeting the plant chloroplast *trnL* (UAA) intron and the ITS2 region, established barcodes for plant identification. Amplicons will be sequenced on an Illumina MiSeq platform. Bioinformatic processing will use the QIIME2 pipeline (pre-2026 version) for quality filtering, denoising with DADA2, and taxonomic assignment against curated reference databases (e.g., EMBL, GenBank). Results will be correlated with concurrent analyses of organic molecular tracers (e.g., levoglucosan, specific plant wax n-alkanes) obtained via solvent extraction and GC-MS.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, complementary framework for high-resolution plant source attribution in atmospheric science. It will directly test the feasibility and limitations of aerosol eDNA metabarcoding as a tool for environmental forensics. The project will generate a unique paired dataset of molecular tracer concentrations and DNA-based taxonomic lists, enabling a critical evaluation of the resolution each method provides for source apportionment models.
proposals.novelty: While eDNA metabarcoding is routine in aquatic and soil ecology, its application to identify plant sources directly from ambient aerosol samples is novel. This approach merges Dr. Simoneit's deep expertise in organic geochemical tracers with cutting-edge (pre-2026) molecular ecology tools to solve a persistent problem in atmospheric aerosol sourcing.

proposals.title: Constraining Brown Carbon Aerosol from Biomass and Coal Combustion: Integrating Molecular Tracer-Based Phenolic Emissions and Optical Properties into the WRF-Chem Regional Model
proposals.motivation: Biomass burning and low-temperature coal combustion are major, yet poorly constrained, sources of light-absorbing organic aerosols, specifically brown carbon (BrC). Phenolic compounds, identified as key molecular tracers for these sources, are known precursors for secondary BrC formation but are not explicitly represented in regional air quality and climate models. This omission leads to significant uncertainty in simulating aerosol radiative forcing and regional haze events.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly incorporating emission fluxes and heterogeneous reaction pathways for specific phenolic compounds (e.g., methoxyphenols, alkylphenols) and their optical properties into a regional atmospheric model will significantly improve the simulated spatial distribution and direct radiative forcing of BrC aerosols.
proposals.method: We will develop a new phenolic compound module for the WRF-Chem model (v4.3, pre-2026). Emission inventories for key phenols will be scaled using Simoneit's established tracer relationships with black carbon and CO from his prior work on resins and lignite. The module will implement a parameterized gas-to-particle partitioning scheme and simplified aging reactions affecting mass absorption efficiency (MAE) at 365 nm. The optical properties (MAE and Ångström exponents) for primary and secondary phenolic BrC will be assigned based on pre-2026 laboratory studies and field measurements, including from the researcher's own work in source regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first regional-scale, tracer-constrained assessment of phenolic BrC impacts on atmospheric absorption. It will deliver a functional, mechanistically informed model parameterization for the community, reducing uncertainty in aerosol radiative forcing estimates over key source regions like East and Southeast Asia.
proposals.novelty: While phenolic compounds are recognized as tracers, their explicit treatment as evolving, optically active chemical species in a 3-D regional model is novel. This bridges the gap between highly specific organic geochemistry and applied atmospheric modeling, moving beyond bulk parameterizations.

proposals.title: Correlating Biomass Lignocellulosic and Trace Metal Composition with Combustion Aerosol Toxicity: A Framework for Engineering Low-Emission Solid Biofuels
proposals.motivation: Solid biomass fuels are a key renewable energy source, but their combustion emits fine particulate matter (PM) containing toxic organic compounds and metals, posing significant public health risks. Pre-2026 research has extensively characterized emissions from sources like coal and waste, but a predictive, compositional framework for proactively designing low-toxicity biomass fuels is lacking. This gap hinders the development of truly sustainable and clean bioenergy systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific, quantifiable relationships exist between the lignocellulosic polymer composition (lignin, cellulose, hemicellulose ratios) and trace metal content of raw biomass and the toxicity profile (PAH, levoglucosan, metal content) of its combustion-generated aerosols. These correlations can be used to engineer or select feedstock blends that minimize harmful emissions.
proposals.method: We will analyze a diverse set of biomass samples (e.g., agricultural residues, energy crops, lignite for comparison) using established methods: pyrolysis-GC/MS for lignocellulosic characterization and ICP-MS for trace metals (Hg, As, Pb, Zn). Controlled combustion experiments in a laboratory furnace will generate PM2.5, which will be analyzed for toxic tracers (PAHs via GC-MS, levoglucosan, speciated metals). Composition-activity relationships will be modeled using multivariate statistical approaches (Principal Component Analysis and Partial Least Squares Regression), building on the applicant's proven expertise, to identify key compositional drivers of aerosol toxicity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, predictive model linking inherent biomass chemistry to emission toxicity, moving beyond descriptive emission inventories. It will deliver a practical screening methodology for selecting or pre-treating biomass feedstocks. The findings will provide a scientific basis for standards on 'low-emission' biofuel design, directly informing sustainable energy policy.
proposals.novelty: While biomass emissions are studied, the proactive engineering of fuels using source-composition-toxicity correlations represents a paradigm shift from post-combustion mitigation. Applying advanced organic geochemical tracer techniques and multivariate modeling, typically used for environmental forensics (e.g., PAH source apportionment), to the predictive design of biofuels is a novel interdisciplinary synthesis.

proposals.title: Refining Primary Organic Aerosol (POA) Source Ratios via Co-located Speciated Organic Tracers and Gaseous Pollutants in Complex Emission Environments
proposals.motivation: Source apportionment of atmospheric aerosols relies on emission ratios of Primary Organic Aerosol (POA) to co-emitted species like elemental carbon (EC), NOx, and CO. Pre-2026, these ratios are often derived from limited source tests and can be highly variable in real-world, mixed-source plumes, leading to significant uncertainty in air quality models and receptor modeling (e.g., Chemical Mass Balance). Validated, environment-specific ratios are critical for accurate source attribution and effective pollution mitigation strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: That the application of an extended dataset of co-located, speciated organic molecular tracers (including biomarkers, PAHs, and hopanes) and concurrent gaseous/aerosol pollutant measurements (EC, NOx, CO) will allow for the resolution and refinement of POA emission ratios specific to dominant source types (e.g., coal combustion, biomass burning, vehicular emissions) in complex urban and industrial environments.
proposals.method: We will collect high-volume air particulate samples and co-located real-time pollutant data (EC, NOx, CO) from contrasting sites influenced by coal mining/processing, urban traffic, and regional biomass burning. Organic compounds will be extracted and analyzed via GC-MS for a comprehensive suite of source-specific tracers (e.g., retene for biomass, hopanes for vehicular, specific PAH ratios for combustion). Positive Matrix Factorization (PMF) and Multilinear Engine (ME-2) algorithms will be applied to the combined tracer and pollutant dataset to separate source contributions. POA/EC, POA/NOx, and POA/CO ratios will be calculated for each resolved source factor and compared to existing literature values.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, tracer-informed set of POA emission ratios for key anthropogenic sources, directly improving the input parameters for regional air quality models like CMAQ. It will demonstrate a robust methodological framework for validating and updating emission factors using ambient data, thereby reducing uncertainty in source apportionment studies and informing more targeted regulatory actions.
proposals.novelty: While POA ratios are commonly used, this study uniquely integrates a broad suite of Dr. Simoneit's expertise in detailed organic tracer analysis with high-time-resolution pollutant measurements to empirically derive and validate these ratios from ambient mixtures, moving beyond controlled source tests to real-world, multi-source complexity.

proposals.title: Integrating Organic Geochemical Source Profiles into a Continental-Scale Chemical Transport Model for PAH and Mercury Source Apportionment in Europe
proposals.motivation: Regional-scale source apportionment studies, such as those for PAHs in the South China Sea or mercury in Polish coal waste, provide critical but geographically limited insights. To inform effective transboundary air quality policy and understand long-range transport of toxic organic compounds and metals, these detailed molecular and isotopic source signatures must be incorporated into continental-scale atmospheric models. This bridges the gap between high-resolution geochemical fingerprinting and macro-scale atmospheric fate and transport.
proposals.hypothesis: That region-specific organic geochemical source profiles—including PAH isomer ratios, biomarkers, and mercury speciation—derived from geological and anthropogenic samples can be successfully integrated into a Eulerian chemical transport model to significantly improve the accuracy of continental-scale source apportionment for persistent toxic substances.
proposals.method: We will compile a harmonized database of source profiles from prior work (e.g., coal lignite, combustion-derived PAHs, petrogenic hydrocarbons) and new analyses of key European source materials. These profiles, quantified as emission speciation factors, will be integrated into the GEOS-Chem chemical transport model (v13.0.0 or later) as new tracers. The model will be configured for a European domain at 0.5° x 0.625° resolution, with emissions from EDGAR and other inventories speciated using our profiles. Model predictions for PAH congeners and mercury species will be validated against long-term monitoring network data (e.g., EMEP) using multivariate statistical techniques (PMF) to deconvolute source contributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first continental-scale model capable of apportioning PAH and mercury concentrations to specific geogenic and anthropogenic source types (e.g., coal waste heating vs. traffic vs. natural oil seeps) across Europe. It will provide a novel, chemistry-explicit framework for evaluating emission control strategies and quantifying transboundary pollution fluxes of these toxic compounds.
proposals.novelty: While CTMs have incorporated simple emission tracers, the systematic integration of complex, multivariate organic geochemical fingerprints—such as biomarker and PAH isomer ratios from geological and industrial samples—into a high-resolution atmospheric model for source apportionment is a novel and untested approach.

proposals.title: Optimizing Urban Emission Reduction Strategies via a Receptor-Model-Informed Inverse Framework: A Case Study Integrating PMF, Chemical Tracers, and Cost-Benefit Analysis
proposals.motivation: Urban air quality management, particularly for particulate matter (PM), often relies on Positive Matrix Factorization (PMF) to identify pollution sources but struggles to translate source apportionment into cost-effective control policies. Pre-2026, there is a critical gap in systematic methodologies that invert PMF-derived source contributions to design prioritized, targeted emission reduction strategies, especially for complex urban-industrial mixes where resources are limited.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating detailed organic compound and inorganic tracer data from PMF with sector-specific emission control cost and efficiency databases will allow the formulation of an inverse optimization model that identifies the minimal-cost combination of control measures to achieve a predetermined air quality target.
proposals.method: The research will employ a three-step technical approach. First, high-resolution PM samples from a target city will be analyzed for a suite of organic markers (e.g., hopanes, steranes, PAHs) and inorganic tracers (e.g., metals, ions) to perform PMF source apportionment. Second, a linear programming optimization framework will be constructed, using PMF source contribution estimates as constraints and incorporating a pre-2026 database of control technology costs and removal efficiencies for identified source sectors (e.g., coal combustion, vehicular traffic, industrial processes). Third, the model will solve the inverse problem to output an optimal control strategy, which will be validated against air quality dispersion modeling scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative decision-support tool that directly links advanced receptor modeling results to actionable policy design. It will advance the field of environmental analytical chemistry beyond source identification into the domain of strategic intervention, offering a replicable methodology for urban planners to achieve air quality goals with maximal economic efficiency.
proposals.novelty: While PMF is a standard diagnostic tool, its systematic inversion into a cost-optimized control strategy is non-obvious and novel, moving from descriptive analytics to prescriptive policy optimization by integrating chemical fingerprinting with economic and engineering parameters in a single computational framework.

proposals.title: Quantitative Apportionment of Urban Particulate Organic Aerosols: A Molecular Tracer-Based Model for Cooking and Vehicular Source Contributions
proposals.motivation: Urban air quality management requires precise source apportionment of particulate organic matter (OM). Pre-2026, while specific molecular tracers for cooking (e.g., cholesterol, specific sterols, and hopanoids from heated oils) and vehicle emissions (e.g., hopanes, steranes, and specific PAHs from lubricating oils and fuel combustion) are established, quantitative models to estimate their precise mass contributions to ambient OM remain underdeveloped. This gap hinders effective policy targeting for emission reduction in rapidly urbanizing regions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multivariate receptor model, incorporating a refined suite of thermally-derived organic tracers and their ratios, can robustly quantify the absolute mass contribution of cooking and vehicular sources to urban particulate OM with lower uncertainty than current methods.
proposals.method: We will collect high-volume aerosol samples from contrasting urban sites. Organic compounds will be extracted, fractionated, and analyzed via GC-MS for target tracers, including hopanes, steranes, PAHs, and specific oxygenated species from cooking. Source profiles will be constructed from controlled experiments (e.g., commercial cooking exhaust, tunnel studies) and literature. The quantitative model will be based on a Chemical Mass Balance (CMB) framework, enhanced with factor analysis (Positive Matrix Factorization, PMF) to resolve collinear sources. Uncertainty will be propagated using Monte Carlo simulations on tracer ratios and concentrations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, tracer-based quantitative model for source apportionment, directly translating molecular marker data into actionable mass contributions. It will provide a methodological advance for environmental forensics and generate a benchmark dataset of source-specific organic tracer profiles applicable to Asian megacities and beyond.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated use of thermally-altered organic tracers (inspired by Simoneit's work on geological and pyrolysis systems) specifically for urban cooking aerosols, combined with a CMB-PMF hybrid approach to deconvolve these collinear combustion sources, moving beyond qualitative or semi-quantitative assessments.

proposals.title: Atmospheric Stability of Biomarker Tracers: Assessing the Photochemical Degradation of Hopanes and Cholesterol for Robust Source Apportionment
proposals.motivation: Hopanes and cholesterol are key organic tracer compounds used for source attribution of atmospheric particulate matter, linking aerosols to fossil fuel combustion and biogenic emissions, respectively. Their utility depends on chemical stability during atmospheric transport, yet pre-2026 knowledge lacks systematic experimental data on their degradation under realistic atmospheric conditions (e.g., UV exposure, oxidants). Unquantified degradation could lead to significant errors in source apportionment models and misinterpretation of emission inventories.
proposals.hypothesis: Hopanes and cholesterol undergo measurable photochemical degradation in the atmosphere on timescales relevant to regional transport, and their degradation products can be identified. The stability of hopanes relative to cholesterol will differ, impacting their reliability as conservative tracers.
proposals.method: We will simulate atmospheric aging using a photochemical flow reactor, exposing standard compounds and NIST urban dust (SRM 1649b) to controlled levels of UV light (simulating solar spectrum) and oxidants (OH, O3). Samples will be collected at timed intervals. Analysis will employ gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC-MS) and two-dimensional gas chromatography–time-of-flight mass spectrometry (GC×GC–TOFMS) to quantify parent compounds and identify transformation products. Degradation kinetics will be modeled using a multivariate statistical approach (Principal Component Analysis) of the full-scan mass spectral data to track compositional changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative degradation rate constants for these critical biomarker tracers under simulated atmospheric conditions. It will establish correction factors for source apportionment studies and identify potential secondary tracer compounds from degradation products, thereby increasing the accuracy of aerosol source attribution in environmental monitoring.
proposals.novelty: While the use of hopanes and cholesterol as tracers is well-established, their fundamental atmospheric chemistry and stability are poorly characterized. This study directly tests the core assumption of their conservative behavior, moving beyond correlation-based source apportionment to a process-based chemical understanding.

proposals.title: Elucidating Complex Organic Signatures in Anthropogenic and Geologic Matrices Using Comprehensive Two-Dimensional Gas Chromatography (GC×GC-TOFMS)
proposals.motivation: Complex organic mixtures from anthropogenic activities (e.g., coal waste) and deep geologic processes (e.g., oil cracking) contain critical tracer compounds that are often co-eluted and obscured in conventional one-dimensional GC analysis. This limits our ability to accurately fingerprint pollution sources and understand diagenetic pathways. Advanced separation is needed to resolve these complex signatures for robust environmental forensics and petroleum geochemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: The application of GC×GC coupled with time-of-flight mass spectrometry (TOFMS) will significantly enhance the separation, detection, and identification of key organic tracers (e.g., specific PAH isomers, biomarkers, organosulfur compounds) in complex environmental and geologic samples, revealing previously unresolved compositional details.
proposals.method: We will apply GC×GC-TOFMS to archived and new sample sets, including lignite, coal waste resins, and marine sediments. A non-polar/mid-polar column set (e.g., Rxi-1ms × Rxi-17Sil MS) will be used for optimal orthogonality. Data processing will utilize ChromaTOF software for peak finding, deconvolution, and library matching (NIST, in-house). Multivariate statistical analysis (PCA, PLS-DA) will correlate resolved chemical profiles with sample origins and processes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a high-fidelity molecular fingerprinting method for environmental and geochemical studies, enabling more precise source apportionment of pollutants and a clearer understanding of in-situ petroleum alteration. It will establish a robust analytical protocol for complex organic mixtures relevant to Dr. Simoneit's research themes.
proposals.novelty: While GC×GC is known, its targeted application to the specific, highly complex matrices central to Simoneit's work (coal waste resins, Precambrian-Cambrian petroleum systems, and tropical marine sediments) to resolve critical but previously masked tracer compounds is novel and addresses clear analytical gaps in his recent publications.

proposals.title: Apportioning Urban Aerosol Sources via Chemical Mass Balance: Adapting Aquatic Receptor Modeling Frameworks Using Organic Tracers and Multivariate Statistics
proposals.motivation: Urban aerosol source apportionment is critical for effective air quality management, yet traditional models often struggle with complex mixtures of organic compounds from combustion (e.g., coal, biomass) and non-combustion sources. Pre-2026, receptor modeling in aquatic chemistry has successfully deconvoluted complex pollutant mixtures in sediments and water using specific molecular tracers and advanced statistics, a toolkit underutilized in atmospheric studies.
proposals.hypothesis: Chemical mass balance (CMB) and multivariate statistical frameworks (e.g., Positive Matrix Factorization - PMF) optimized for aquatic systems can be successfully adapted to urban aerosol datasets, providing improved resolution of contributing sources, especially for organic aerosols derived from coal processing, traffic, and waste emissions.
proposals.method: We will collect PM2.5 samples from an urban-industrial area and analyze for a suite of organic tracers, including polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), hopanes, steranes, and specific lignin-derived phenols, building on Simoneit's expertise. The core approach adapts aquatic CMB/PMF receptor modeling protocols (e.g., from sediment source apportionment) to the aerosol matrix. This involves defining source profiles from local emitters (traffic, coal, residential heating) and applying the US EPA CMB 8.2 and PMF 5.0 models to quantify contributions, using diagnostic ratios and statistical validation from aquatic studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, tracer-rich quantitative source apportionment for urban aerosols, directly linking particulate pollution to specific local and regional emitters. It will establish a methodological bridge between aquatic and atmospheric geochemistry, expanding the toolkit for environmental forensics. The findings will offer actionable data for targeting emission controls in complex urban environments.
proposals.novelty: The explicit translation of well-established aquatic receptor modeling protocols and their associated multivariate statistical rigor to urban aerosol science is novel. Leveraging detailed organic tracer suites, common in Simoneit's work on sediments and coals, for atmospheric CMB/PMF modeling represents a significant cross-disciplinary methodological innovation.

proposals.title: Validation of Industrial and Urban Emission Inventories Using Molecular Tracer Ratios in Ambient Particulate Matter
proposals.motivation: Bottom-up emission inventories are critical for air quality management and climate modeling, but their accuracy is often uncertain due to incomplete source profiles and activity data. Pre-2026 studies, including those on coal waste dumps and marine sediments, demonstrate that specific organic compounds and inorganic species serve as robust tracers for complex emission sources. A systematic, field-based validation of inventory-predicted tracer ratios is needed to reduce uncertainties in source apportionment and regulatory policy.
proposals.hypothesis: The ratios of key organic (e.g., hopanes, PAHs) and inorganic (e.g., Hg species, trace metals) tracers measured in ambient PM samples will show statistically significant deviations from the ratios predicted by current regional emission inventories, revealing systematic biases in source characterization.
proposals.method: We will collect high-volume ambient PM samples from receptor sites downwind of major industrial and urban sources. Samples will be analyzed for a suite of tracers, including Hg species, PAHs, hopanes, and resin-derived diterpenoids, using GC-MS and ICP-MS. Bottom-up ratios will be calculated by aggregating source-specific emission factors from inventories (e.g., EMEP, EDGAR). The validation will employ multivariate statistics (Principal Component Analysis and Multiple Linear Regression) to compare measured vs. predicted tracer ratios and quantify discrepancies, with a focus on coal combustion, vehicular, and biomass burning sources.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, tracer-based assessment of emission inventory accuracy, identifying which source categories are most poorly characterized. It will deliver a refined set of tracer ratios for future source apportionment studies and offer direct feedback for improving the chemical speciation profiles used in inventory development.
proposals.novelty: While tracer ratios are used for source identification, their systematic application as a validation metric for bottom-up emission inventories, combining Simoneit's expertise in complex organic geochemistry with inorganic toxic species like Hg, represents a novel, dual-marker approach to benchmarking regulatory tools.

proposals.title: Quantifying Indoor Source-Reservoir Dynamics of Semivolatile Organic Compounds (SVOCs) Using Stable Isotope-Labeled Tracers
proposals.motivation: Indoor environments are major reservoirs for SVOCs like phthalates and flame retardants, which partition onto surfaces, creating persistent secondary sources. Pre-2026 research lacks quantitative tools to trace the dynamic exchange of these compounds between sources (e.g., materials) and indoor reservoirs (e.g., dust, surfaces), hindering accurate exposure assessment and source apportionment. This gap limits the development of effective mitigation strategies for these ubiquitous contaminants.
proposals.hypothesis: Stable isotope-labeled SVOC analogs, deployed in controlled indoor environments, can be used to trace and quantify the kinetics of reservoir exchange and to apportion the contributions of primary sources versus re-emissions from reservoirs to indoor air concentrations.
proposals.method: We will synthesize or procure stable isotope-labeled (e.g., 13C or D) analogs of high-priority SVOCs (e.g., DEHP). In controlled chamber studies, we will introduce these labeled tracers to simulate emissions from a primary source. Concurrently, we will collect time-series samples of air, settled dust, and wipe samples from various surfaces (using pre-cleaned substrates). Compound-specific isotope analysis via Gas Chromatography-Isotope Ratio Mass Spectrometry (GC-IRMS) will distinguish the labeled tracer from background, unlabeled SVOCs. A multi-compartment mass balance model, informed by the tracer data, will be developed to quantify exchange rate constants and source contributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative framework for tracing SVOC fate indoors, moving beyond equilibrium assumptions. It will yield novel kinetic data on source-to-reservoir exchange for key SVOC classes. The methodology will establish a new experimental paradigm for validating and refining indoor exposure models, directly informing risk assessment and material policy.
proposals.novelty: While isotopic tracers are established in atmospheric chemistry for tracking reaction pathways, their application to trace physical partitioning and reservoir dynamics in indoor environments is novel. This approach directly quantifies the 'recycling' of SVOCs from indoor reservoirs, a hypothesized but poorly quantified process critical for understanding long-term exposure.

proposals.title: Kinetics and Mechanisms of Atmospheric Oxidation of a Key Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbon Tracer Ion: A Controlled Chamber Study
proposals.motivation: Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and their tracer ions are crucial for source apportionment in environmental forensics, as seen in Simoneit's work on South China Sea sediments. However, their utility as conservative tracers is compromised by atmospheric processing, which remains poorly quantified mechanistically. Establishing the chemical stability and transformation pathways of specific tracer ions under controlled oxidative conditions is essential to validate their use in long-range transport models and retrospective source assessments.
proposals.hypothesis: The specific POA tracer ion will exhibit distinct, quantifiable degradation pathways and rate constants when exposed to major atmospheric oxidants (OH, O3, NO3), and its stability will be significantly altered when adsorbed onto particulate matter compared to its pure form.
proposals.method: The pure tracer compound and tracer-doped onto relevant particulate substrates (e.g., silica, soot) will be introduced into a state-of-the-art atmospheric simulation chamber. Controlled exposures to generated OH radicals (via HONO photolysis), O3, and NO3 radicals (via N2O5 thermal decomposition) will be conducted. Molecular transformation will be tracked in real-time using a High-Resolution Time-of-Flight Chemical Ionization Mass Spectrometer (HR-ToF-CIMS) with acetate ionization for detailed product identification. Pseudo-first-order rate constants will be derived from decay curves, and product spectra will be analyzed using multivariate statistical techniques (e.g., Positive Matrix Factorization) to elucidate dominant reaction pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first laboratory-derived kinetic parameters and mechanistic pathways for the atmospheric degradation of a critical PAH tracer ion. It will directly inform the interpretation of field data by quantifying the tracer's robustness, thereby improving the reliability of source apportionment in complex environments like coal waste sites and marine sediments studied by Simoneit.
proposals.novelty: While Simoneit's prior work identifies sources using tracers, this proposal shifts to a foundational, process-level experimental study that mechanistically quantifies the tracer's atmospheric fate, moving beyond correlation-based field observations to establish causative kinetic data.

proposals.title: Assessing the Stability and Volatility of the Benzo[ghi]perylene Coronene (BghiP/Cor) Tracer Ion Under Simulated Atmospheric Oxidation: Implications for Source Apportionment
proposals.motivation: The benzo[ghi]perylene coronene (BghiP/Cor) ion is widely used as a conservative tracer for pyrogenic carbon in atmospheric source apportionment studies, assuming it is inert and non-volatile. However, pre-2026 studies on polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and their derivatives show they can degrade under atmospheric aging. This assumption, if flawed, introduces significant error in quantifying fossil fuel versus biomass burning contributions to organic aerosols.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the BghiP/Cor tracer ion is not completely inert and can undergo oxidative degradation or exhibit semi-volatile partitioning under high oxidant loads (e.g., OH, O3, NO3), challenging its reliability as a conservative tracer in aged air masses.
proposals.method: We will use a Potential Aerosol Mass (PAM) oxidation flow reactor coupled to a High-Resolution Time-of-Flight Aerosol Mass Spectrometer (HR-ToF-AMS) and a Thermal Desorption Aerosol Gas Chromatograph (TAG). Laboratory-generated soot samples, including lignite and resin combustion products, will be subjected to controlled, high concentrations of atmospheric oxidants. The method will track the decay of the BghiP/Cor ion (m/z 276) relative to other PAH markers and bulk organics, using multivariate statistical approaches (e.g., Positive Matrix Factorization) to deconvolve signals. Volatility will be assessed via thermodenuder experiments integrated into the flow system.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic experimental constraints on the atmospheric stability of a key POA tracer ion. It will deliver a quantitative correction factor or identify conditions where its use is invalid, thereby improving the accuracy of source apportionment models for organic aerosols. The findings will directly impact interpretations of field data from polluted regions like East Asia and Europe.
proposals.novelty: While PAH reactivity is studied, the specific inertness of the BghiP/Cor *tracer ion* used in AMS-based models is an untested foundational assumption. This study uniquely targets this operational marker under simulated aging, bridging compound-specific organic geochemistry and real-time aerosol mass spectrometry.

proposals.title: Constraining VOC Emission Scaling: A Tracer-Based Fingerprinting Approach for Fuel-Specific Secondary Organic Aerosol Precursor Inventories
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of secondary organic aerosol (SOA) formation from combustion sources requires detailed knowledge of volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions. Current atmospheric models often scale complex VOC inventories using a single, easily measured tracer like toluene, but this assumes a universal emission profile across all fuel types. This assumption is untested and likely flawed, given known variability in fuel composition and combustion conditions, leading to significant uncertainty in SOA source apportionment and climate models.
proposals.hypothesis: The emission ratios of major VOC classes (e.g., aromatics, alkanes, alkenes, oxygenates) to a conserved tracer like toluene are fuel-specific. A database of these 'chemical fingerprints' can be used to accurately predict full VOC emission profiles from field measurements of the tracer, provided the fuel source is identified.
proposals.method: Phase 1: Controlled combustion experiments will be conducted on a diverse fuel suite (including lignite, hard coal, biomass, and synthetic resins) using a laboratory-scale furnace. Emissions will be collected on adsorbent tubes and analyzed by thermal desorption coupled with comprehensive two-dimensional gas chromatography–time-of-flight mass spectrometry (GC×GC–TOFMS). Phase 2: A field campaign at a known combustion source (e.g., a coal waste dump or prescribed burn) will measure ambient toluene (as the tracer) and the full VOC suite. Fuel-specific emission ratios from the laboratory database will be applied to the field toluene concentration to predict other VOC levels, which will be validated against direct field measurements using multivariate statistical analysis (e.g., Principal Component Analysis).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic evaluation of the toluene-scaling assumption across fuel types, quantifying its uncertainties. It will deliver a validated, practical framework for deriving detailed VOC/SOA precursor inventories from limited field data, directly improving the accuracy of regional air quality and climate models. The methodology bridges controlled laboratory source characterization and field-based atmospheric chemistry.
proposals.novelty: The approach innovatively adapts the concept of conserved biomarker tracers and multi-class chemical fingerprinting from petroleum and environmental forensics (Simoneit's expertise) to the problem of atmospheric emission scaling, moving beyond a single-tracer paradigm. It explicitly tests a widespread modeling assumption that has not been rigorously validated against a comprehensive fuel database.

proposals.title: Quantifying Synergistic and Inhibitory Interactions in Fuel-Specific Secondary Organic Aerosol Formation from Complex Precursor Mixtures
proposals.motivation: Secondary organic aerosol (SOA) is a major component of atmospheric particulate matter, impacting climate and health. Current atmospheric models often treat SOA formation from fuel combustion using simplified, additive precursor contributions, failing to capture the complex, nonlinear chemistry observed in real-world emissions. A mechanistic understanding of how interactions within a fuel's unique blend of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) govern ultimate aerosol yield is critically needed to improve model predictive power for air quality and climate forcing.
proposals.hypothesis: The SOA yield from a combustion fuel is an emergent property of its specific precursor mixture, dictated by key interactive chemical processes—including OH radical recycling, cross-reactions of peroxy radicals (RO2), and competition for oxidants—that synergistically enhance or inhibit aerosol mass formation in a non-additive manner.
proposals.method: We will employ controlled oxidation flow reactor (OFR) experiments on authentic fuel vapors (e.g., from lignite, coal, and biomass pyrolysis) and synthetic mixtures replicating their detailed VOC spectra (including aromatics, alkanes, alkenes). Real-time, high-resolution chemical characterization will use Proton-Transfer-Reaction Time-of-Flight Mass Spectrometry (PTR-ToF-MS) and Filter Inlet for Gases and AEROsols (FIGAERO-CIMS). Data will be analyzed using multivariate statistics (e.g., Positive Matrix Factorization) to deconvolve sources, and a chemical box model incorporating the Master Chemical Mechanism (MCM) will be explicitly extended with cross-RO2 reaction pathways and product uptake coefficients to quantify interaction terms and validate a parameterizable yield framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, mechanistic framework that moves beyond descriptive fuel classification to predict SOA yield based on a fuel's precursor spectrum and the quantified strength of key interactive processes. It will provide critical, experimentally validated parameters (interaction coefficients) for next-generation chemical transport models, significantly reducing uncertainty in anthropogenic SOA simulations.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have identified specific precursor classes, this research uniquely focuses on the *interactions* between them as the governing factor for SOA yield, integrating detailed molecular-level tracers of fuel combustion with explicit mechanistic modeling of multi-precursor chemistry.

proposals.title: Fuel-Specific Molecular Tracers and VOC Signatures to Constrain Secondary Organic Aerosol (SOA) Formation from Global Biomass Burning
proposals.motivation: Secondary organic aerosol (SOA) from biomass burning significantly impacts climate and air quality, but current models have large uncertainties due to oversimplified volatile organic compound (VOC) emission profiles. Pre-2026 inventories often treat biomass fuels as broad categories, lacking the molecular specificity needed to predict SOA yields from diverse, real-world fuels like agricultural residues, peat, and specific wood types, whose combustion emits distinct VOC suites.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that constructing a fuel-specific VOC emission inventory, anchored by diagnostic molecular tracers (e.g., lignin pyrolysis products, resin acids, specific polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons), will significantly improve the mechanistic prediction of SOA mass and composition in regional air quality models.
