proposals.method: We will compile and analyze existing pre-2026 data from lab combustion studies and field campaigns (e.g., FIREX, BBOP) to build a fuel-classified VOC database. For key under-sampled fuels, controlled pyrolysis-GC/MS experiments will be conducted to quantify VOC and tracer emissions. These fuel-specific VOC profiles will be integrated into a 2D-VBS (Volatility Basis Set) model framework. Model validation will use multivariate statistics (PCA, PMF) on ambient datasets from regions with known fuel types, comparing predicted vs. observed SOA tracers and concentrations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a publicly accessible, expanded fuel-specific VOC emission parameterization for SOA models. It will directly improve the accuracy of regional air quality forecasts in fire-prone areas and provide a tracer-based framework to apportion SOA sources in complex environments like the South China Sea or industrial-urban mixes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly linking detailed, fuel-specific organic geochemical tracer data (a core expertise of the researcher) with modern SOA modeling parameterizations, moving beyond bulk NMOC emissions to a molecularly-defined approach for key, climate-relevant biomass fuels.

proposals.title: Observationally-Constrained Scaling of Fuel-Specific SOA Yields in Regional Models Using the POA Tracer Ion Method
proposals.motivation: Secondary organic aerosol (SOA) is a major component of fine particulate matter, impacting climate and air quality, but its formation in regional atmospheric models remains highly uncertain. Current models rely on parameterizations from chamber studies, which may not accurately represent complex, real-world atmospheric conditions and multi-phase chemistry. This discrepancy leads to significant biases in predicting SOA concentrations and their climate forcing, particularly from key anthropogenic and biomass burning sources.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the bias between modeled and ambient-measured SOA production for specific fuel sources is systematic and can be quantified using the POA tracer ion method, enabling the development of empirical correction factors to improve model parameterizations.
proposals.method: We will deploy aerosol mass spectrometers to collect high-resolution organic aerosol and tracer ion data in diverse real-world plumes (e.g., urban, biomass burning, coal combustion). The POA tracer ion method will be used to apportion primary organic aerosol (POA) sources and estimate the corresponding co-emitted SOA precursor mass. This provides an observational benchmark for SOA production. We will then compare these observations to SOA predictions from a regional model (e.g., WRF-Chem) using standard chamber-derived parameterizations. For each major fuel source, a bias metric (observed SOA / modeled SOA) will be calculated. Finally, we will develop and apply source-specific, multiplicative correction factors to the model's volatility basis set (VBS) or two-dimensional VBS (2D-VBS) yields before model integration.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first set of observationally-derived, fuel-specific correction factors for SOA parameterizations, directly reducing model uncertainty. It will create a novel, scalable framework for constraining regional and global models with ambient validation data. The improved model will provide more reliable predictions of SOA climate impacts and inform more effective air quality management strategies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in fusing the POA tracer ion method, an observational tool, directly into the model parameterization process to create an observationally-constrained scaling pathway, rather than just performing a posterior model evaluation. This proactive integration of ambient data to correct fundamental chamber-derived yields before model implementation is a significant advance.

proposals.title: Field Validation of the POA Tracer Ion Method for Quantifying Secondary Organic Aerosol in Complex Atmospheric Plumes
proposals.motivation: Quantifying secondary organic aerosol (SOA) in ambient air remains a major analytical challenge, crucial for understanding air quality and climate impacts. Current methods, including tracer-based and molecular marker approaches, often rely on assumptions about source profiles and aging that are difficult to verify in complex, real-world plumes. A robust internal standard method for SOA quantification is needed to improve source apportionment accuracy, particularly in mixed urban-industrial and biomass burning environments.
proposals.hypothesis: The POA (Primary Organic Aerosol) tracer ion method, previously applied in source studies and controlled experiments, can serve as a stable internal standard for quantifying SOA mass in dynamic atmospheric plumes when coupled with high-resolution aerosol mass spectrometry and multivariate statistics.
proposals.method: We will deploy a High-Resolution Time-of-Flight Aerosol Mass Spectrometer (HR-ToF-AMS) and complementary gas-phase monitors to sample plumes from defined sources (e.g., traffic, coal combustion, biomass burning) and aged regional air at contrasting field sites, including a polluted coastal region influenced by shipping and urban emissions. The method involves: 1) Identifying and quantifying a conserved, source-specific POA tracer ion (e.g., m/z 60 for biomass burning, m/z 57 for hydrocarbon-like OA) in fresh plumes. 2) Applying Positive Matrix Factorization (PMF) to the full AMS mass spectral dataset to resolve SOA factors. 3) Testing the hypothesis by using the decay ratio of the selected POA tracer ion relative to its fresh source concentration as an internal standard to constrain the absolute mass of the co-evolved SOA, correcting for dilution and photochemical aging.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a critical field validation of a promising quantitative method for SOA, moving it from controlled settings to real-world complexity. It will yield a more constrained, tracer-anchored estimate of SOA loadings in atmospheric models. The results will directly improve the interpretation of organic aerosol source apportionment studies in regions with mixed pollution sources.
proposals.novelty: While POA tracer ions are established for source identification, their rigorous application as an internal standard for *quantifying* SOA mass in ambient, temporally evolving plumes is novel. This bridges the gap between controlled laboratory tracer studies and the need for robust quantification in field campaigns, leveraging Dr. Simoneit's expertise in organic tracers and complex environmental samples.

proposals.title: A Common-Atmosphere Robotic Sequential Sampling System for High-Throughput, Direct Comparison of Organic Compound Reactivity and Secondary Aerosol Formation
proposals.motivation: Current comparative studies of fuel reactivity and secondary organic aerosol (SOA) formation potential are hampered by inter-chamber variability in parallel reactor systems, introducing significant uncertainty. This flaw impedes the accurate ranking of emission sources and the validation of chemical tracers used in source apportionment, a core theme in environmental organic geochemistry. A method guaranteeing identical oxidative conditions for all samples is critically needed to resolve these comparative uncertainties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single, master atmospheric chamber with a uniform, highly controlled reactive environment, coupled with sequential robotic sampling of multiple fuel vessels, will yield direct, high-fidelity comparisons of reaction kinetics and SOA yields that are impossible with parallel chamber setups.
proposals.method: We will construct a master Teflon environmental chamber with precise control of UV flux (using calibrated xenon arc lamps), temperature, humidity, and OH radical concentration (via HONO photolysis or ozone-alkene reactions). An array of up to 50 miniature glass sample vessels, containing diverse solid and liquid fuel samples (e.g., lignite, coal waste, resins, crude oil fractions), will be placed inside. A robotic sampling arm, guided by a pre-programmed sequence, will sequentially pierce vessel septa, extract headspace gas via a heated transfer line, and deliver it to a Proton-Transfer-Reaction Time-of-Flight Mass Spectrometer (PTR-TOF-MS) and a Scanning Mobility Particle Sizer (SMPS) for real-time analysis of volatile organic compounds and aerosol size distributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-throughput platform for directly comparing the atmospheric reactivity of complex organic mixtures under perfectly identical conditions. It will generate a robust, internally consistent dataset on SOA formation potentials from diverse fuels, refining the use of specific organic compounds and ratios as environmental tracers. The methodology will set a new standard for experimental comparability in atmospheric oxidation studies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging the principle of a single, uniform reaction environment—eliminating inter-chamber variability—with automated, sequential high-frequency sampling. This hybrid approach is distinct from existing parallel chamber or flow tube systems, enabling true apples-to-apples comparisons of multiple samples in a single experiment for the first time.

proposals.title: Deconvolving Mixed Organic Aerosol Sources in Complex Environments: Application of Spatial-Temporal Independent Component Analysis to High-Resolution Aerosol Mass Spectrometry
proposals.motivation: Accurate source apportionment of organic aerosols (OA) is critical for understanding air quality and climate impacts, yet traditional Positive Matrix Factorization (PMF) can struggle with complex, dynamic mixtures where source profiles are non-stationary or highly correlated. This is particularly challenging for separating primary (POA) and secondary (SOA) organic aerosols in environments influenced by multiple combustion sources and atmospheric processing, a core theme in Simoneit's prior work on organic tracers. A more robust, data-driven separation method is needed to resolve these overlapping signatures without strong a priori constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Blind Source Separation (BSS) techniques, specifically spatial-temporal Independent Component Analysis (ICA) as used in neuroimaging, can statistically deconvolve Aerodyne Aerosol Mass Spectrometer (AMS) data into more physically meaningful independent components representing distinct OA sources and processes than standard PMF, by leveraging higher-order statistical independence beyond covariance.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Infomax or FastICA algorithm, established in fMRI for separating neural signals from artifacts, to the deconvolution of unit-mass-resolution AMS spectral matrices (m/z vs. time). The time series of mass spectra will be treated as a linear mixture of statistically independent source components. The spatial-temporal ICA framework will be implemented, where 'spatial' refers to the mass spectrum (m/z space) of each component and 'temporal' to its time series. The algorithm will be tested and validated using both synthetic datasets and real-world AMS data from complex environments, such as urban and biomass-burning-influenced sites, with comparisons to PMF and known tracer compounds.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, open-source analytical framework for OA source apportionment that is less sensitive to user-selected initial conditions and rotational ambiguity than PMF. It will yield a more objective separation of co-varying sources (e.g., different combustion POA types) and chemically dynamic SOA factors, directly advancing the interpretation of complex organic aerosol mixtures in atmospheric science.
proposals.novelty: The direct adaptation of spatial-temporal ICA from neuroimaging to AMS data analysis is a novel cross-disciplinary application. It moves beyond the second-order statistics of PMF by seeking statistically independent components, a fundamentally different constraint not yet widely explored for AMS deconvolution.

proposals.title: Chemometric Deconvolution of Aerosol Mass Spectrometer Data: A Multivariate Approach to Quantify Primary and Secondary Organic Aerosol Sources
proposals.motivation: Accurate source apportionment of organic aerosols (OA) is critical for air quality and climate models. The Aerodyne Aerosol Mass Spectrometer (AMS) provides high-resolution spectral data, but the separation of primary OA (POA) from secondary OA (SOA) remains challenging due to spectral overlap and chemical evolution. Improved deconvolution of AMS spectra is needed to better constrain source contributions and atmospheric processing, informing effective pollution mitigation strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that multivariate statistical methods, specifically Positive Matrix Factorization (PMF) coupled with advanced factor rotation techniques and multi-linear engine (ME-2) analysis, can robustly deconvolve AMS spectra to resolve distinct POA (e.g., biomass burning, fossil fuel) and SOA (e.g., oxidized, aged) factors that are more chemically and physically meaningful than current solutions.
proposals.method: We will apply chemometric techniques, including Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for initial dimensionality reduction, followed by PMF with the Igor Pro-based toolkit. The analysis will utilize the ME-2 solver to apply explicit constraints on factor profiles (e.g., from laboratory spectra or known tracers). Key algorithms include the multilinear engine and bootstrap resampling for uncertainty estimation. The approach will be tested on existing AMS datasets from diverse environments (urban, marine, forested) to validate factor separation against independent tracers (e.g., levoglucosan for biomass burning, m/z 44 for oxygenation).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a refined, standardized chemometric framework for AMS data analysis, leading to more accurate quantification of POA and SOA source strengths. It will contribute directly to improving regional and global aerosol source inventories and advance understanding of SOA formation pathways by isolating more chemically specific factor profiles.
proposals.novelty: While PMF is commonly used in AMS analysis, the novelty lies in the systematic integration of advanced factor constraint protocols from ME-2 analysis and the application of rigorous multivariate statistical validation techniques, akin to those used in Dr. Simoneit's prior work on sediment hydrocarbons, to address the persistent ambiguity in separating closely correlated OA sources.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Impact of Evolving Gasoline Fleet Emissions on VOC Reactivity and Secondary Organic Aerosol Formation: A Dynamic Source-Resolved Oxidation Chamber Study
proposals.motivation: Projected changes in vehicle technology and fuel composition will alter the molecular fingerprint of urban VOC emissions, but current atmospheric models rely on static source profiles. This creates significant uncertainty in predicting future air quality, as the non-linear chemical reactivity of evolving VOC mixtures and their secondary organic aerosol (SOA) yields are not well-constrained by experimental data. A dynamic, source-resolved experimental approach is needed to directly link emission evolution to atmospheric oxidation processes.
proposals.hypothesis: The real-time chemical reactivity (OH reactivity, SOA yield) of a complex VOC mixture in an oxidation chamber is a non-linear function of its dynamic source composition, and technology-driven shifts in the emission profile (e.g., increased aromatic vs. alkane ratios) will significantly alter radical budgets and secondary product formation.
proposals.method: We will generate dynamic, time-resolved VOC mixtures using a novel blending system fed by real-world, speciated emission data from gasoline vehicles of varying technology vintages. These mixtures will be introduced into a state-of-the-art oxidation chamber (e.g., potential aerosol mass reactor). Fast, online process gas chromatography (e.g., GC-MS/FID) and proton-transfer-reaction mass spectrometry (PTR-MS) will monitor the decay of individual VOCs and the evolution of oxidation products in real time. Multivariate statistical approaches, including positive matrix factorization (PMF), will be applied to deconvolute the reactivity contributions from different source-influenced chemical classes (e.g., light alkanes, aromatics, intermediate volatility compounds).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first experimentally derived, time-resolved functions linking dynamic source profiles to VOC reactivity and SOA yields. It will provide a crucial dataset to validate and improve chemical transport models used for future air quality projections. The results will directly inform policy on the atmospheric impacts of fleet turnover and fuel formulation changes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond static single-source or surrogate VOC experiments by using dynamically blended, real-world emission profiles as chamber inputs, allowing for the direct experimental quantification of reactivity evolution from source change, a process previously only modeled.

proposals.title: Biomass Fuel Source Attribution for Aged Atmospheric Aerosols via High-Resolution Mass Spectrometry and Pattern Recognition
proposals.motivation: Accurately apportioning sources of aged, oxidized organic aerosols remains a critical challenge in atmospheric chemistry and climate modeling. Current molecular tracer methods often fail for highly processed particles, limiting our understanding of regional biomass burning impacts and hindering effective pollution mitigation strategies. This gap necessitates novel forensic approaches to extract source information from degraded chemical mixtures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, thermally stable molecular fragments (e.g., from lignin, cellulose, or specific plant lipids) survive atmospheric oxidation and retain a diagnostic signature of their original biomass fuel type (e.g., coniferous vs. deciduous wood, crop residues, peat).
proposals.method: We will generate a reference library by conducting controlled combustion and smog-chamber aging experiments on major fuel types. Ultrahigh-resolution mass spectrometry (FT-ICR MS or Orbitrap) will be used to characterize the complex molecular mixtures, focusing on persistent, fuel-specific features in the m/z space. Pattern recognition will be performed using a Random Forest classifier, trained on the reference spectral fingerprints, to deconvolve fuel sources in complex field samples from diverse environments, such as marine sediments or ice cores, which archive aged aerosol deposition.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new analytical framework for source apportionment of severely processed organic aerosols, moving beyond traditional tracer compounds. It will provide a tool to reconstruct historical fire regimes and fuel use from environmental archives, directly linking to Dr. Simoneit's expertise in organic geochemistry of sediments and pollutant tracers.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct adaptation of forensic proteomics' 'inverse problem' logic—identifying source from degraded patterns—to atmospheric aerosol chemistry, a conceptual crossover not previously attempted. It leverages high-resolution mass spectrometry's untargeted capability in a targeted, machine-learning-driven manner for source attribution.

proposals.title: A Mass-Balance Accounting Framework to Quantify the Competing Roles of Secondary Organic Aerosol Production and Primary Organic Aerosol Volatilization
proposals.motivation: The net concentration of organic aerosol (OA) in the atmosphere results from competing processes: secondary organic aerosol (SOA) production from oxidation of volatile organic compounds and the volatilization loss of primary organic aerosol (POA). Current models struggle to accurately represent OA evolution because they often treat these processes independently, lacking a constrained framework to quantify their simultaneous, opposing effects. This leads to persistent uncertainties in predicting aerosol climate forcing and air quality impacts.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying a dual-entry mass-balance accounting constraint, analogous to a financial balance sheet, the net observed change in OA mass can be explicitly partitioned into the 'revenue' of SOA production and the 'expense' of POA volatilization, allowing for the direct quantification of POA loss rates that suppress net OA accumulation.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework using speciated organic tracer data (e.g., hopanes, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, oxidation markers) from field campaigns and laboratory experiments to define conserved mass pools. The core algorithm will solve the mass-balance equation: ΔOA_observed = Σ(SOA production tracers * yield factors) – Σ(POA volatility loss tracers * evaporation rates). This will be implemented using a multivariate statistical model (e.g., Positive Matrix Factorization combined with volatility basis set distributions) to deconvolve the sources and sinks. The framework will be tested against ensemble model outputs and controlled chamber data to constrain the uncertainty in the solved fluxes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first explicit, observationally constrained method to quantify POA volatilization losses in ambient air, a process critical for accurate OA modeling. It will deliver a novel diagnostic tool to evaluate and improve chemical transport models by reconciling top-down and bottom-up OA estimates. The framework will advance our mechanistic understanding of OA lifecycle, directly informing climate and air quality policy.
proposals.novelty: The conceptual innovation is the adaptation of a rigorous financial accounting identity to atmospheric mass balance, forcing an explicit, simultaneous solution for competing OA source and sink terms. This moves beyond correlative tracer studies to a causative, constrained system analysis.

proposals.title: Compound-Specific Stable Carbon Isotope (δ13C) Fingerprinting of n-Alkanes to Quantify Terrestrial Biogenic versus Fossil Fuel Contributions in Urban Coastal Sediments
proposals.motivation: Urbanized coastal sediments are sinks for complex mixtures of organic matter, including petrogenic hydrocarbons and terrestrial plant waxes (n-alkanes). Pre-2026, source apportionment often relies on molecular indices (e.g., CPI, LMW/HMW), which can be ambiguous when sources are mixed or degraded. A more robust, quantitative tracer is needed to accurately partition biogenic and anthropogenic inputs for environmental forensics and carbon cycling models.
proposals.hypothesis: The δ13C signatures of long-chain n-alkanes (C25–C33) derived from modern C3 terrestrial vegetation are significantly more depleted (lighter) than those from thermally mature fossil fuels (e.g., coal, crude oil), allowing for quantitative source apportionment in environmental mixtures.
proposals.method: Sediment core samples from an urban-impacted estuary (e.g., South China Sea) will be extracted with dichloromethane, and the aliphatic fraction separated via silica gel chromatography. n-Alkanes will be isolated by urea adduction. Compound-specific δ13C analysis will be performed using gas chromatography-isotope ratio mass spectrometry (GC-IRMS). Source apportionment will be calculated using a Bayesian isotopic mixing model (e.g., MixSIAR) informed by pre-2026 end-member δ13C databases for regional C3 plants (-32‰ to -39‰) and relevant fossil fuels (e.g., lignite, crude oil: -24‰ to -28‰).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for distinguishing modern biogenic carbon from fossil carbon in complex environmental matrices. It will directly advance the field of organic geochemistry by moving beyond qualitative indices to isotope-based mass balance, with applications for tracing anthropogenic impacts and understanding the fate of terrestrial organic matter in marine systems.
proposals.novelty: While CSIA is established for light hydrocarbons and PAHs, its systematic application to source-apportion long-chain n-alkanes in coastal sediments, integrating the researcher's expertise in both terrestrial biomarkers (lignite) and anthropogenic tracers, represents a novel synthesis. The use of Bayesian mixing models for this specific compound class in environmental forensics is an innovative quantitative step.

proposals.title: Reconstructing Urban Canopy Composition from Aerosol-Bound Biomarkers: A Chemometric Inverse Modeling Approach
proposals.motivation: Urban vegetation is a critical component of ecosystem services and public health, but city-wide, species-level canopy surveys are resource-intensive. Concurrently, ambient aerosols are known to carry a complex mixture of organic compounds, including specific biomarkers from local flora (e.g., terpenoids, n-alkanes, lignin phenols). This presents an opportunity to develop a novel, atmospheric-chemistry-based proxy for urban biodiversity and vegetation mapping.
proposals.hypothesis: The assemblage of plant-derived molecular tracers (biomarkers) in urban aerosol samples contains sufficient chemical information to inversely model and reconstruct the relative abundance of major plant functional types (e.g., conifers, broadleaf trees, grasses) within the urban canopy.
proposals.method: We will collect size-segregated aerosol samples from multiple urban sites across seasons. Organic compounds will be extracted and analyzed via GC-MS, targeting a suite of known plant biomarkers (e.g., terpenes, n-alkanes, alcohols, sterols, lignin monomers). Following Simoneit's expertise, we will apply multivariate statistical approaches, including Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and Positive Matrix Factorization (PMF), to deconvolve source profiles. The inverse problem will be solved using a constrained linear mixing model, where the measured aerosol chemical matrix is regressed against a library of source-specific biomarker ratios derived from local plant species.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational methodology for 'aerosol forensics' of urban flora, providing a cost-effective tool for monitoring green infrastructure and its phenological changes. It will expand the application of organic geochemistry and chemometrics from traditional environmental source apportionment (e.g., for PAHs) to active ecological sensing.
proposals.novelty: While biomarkers are used to trace vegetation in paleo-records and regional aerosols, applying this as an inverse modeling tool for real-time, quantitative reconstruction of a living urban canopy is a novel and non-obvious application. It inverts the typical source-to-sink paradigm to use the sink (aerosol) as a quantitative recorder of the source (vegetation).

proposals.title: Constraining the Atmospheric Fate of Coal-Derived Organic Tracers: A Combined Field and Laboratory Study of Photochemical Degradation and Gas-Particle Partitioning
proposals.motivation: The long-range atmospheric transport of organic compounds from sources like coal waste dumps and industrial regions is often modeled assuming inert behavior, potentially overestimating their environmental persistence and geographical reach. Pre-2026 studies, including Simoneit's work on HgS, MeHg, and PAHs, have identified high concentrations of toxic organics at source points, but their chemical transformation during transport remains poorly quantified. Understanding the competing processes of photochemical degradation and gas-particle partitioning is critical for accurate risk assessment of these pollutants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the atmospheric transport of specific organic tracer compounds (e.g., specific PAHs, alkylated aromatics, and organosulfur species) from coal-related sources is non-conservative, with their environmental signatures being significantly altered by wavelength-dependent photodegradation and temperature-driven repartitioning between gas and particulate phases during transit.
proposals.method: We will collect high-volume air samples along a transect downwind of a documented coal-waste emission source (e.g., Upper Silesia, Poland) using resin-based sorbents (XAD/PUF) and quartz fiber filters to separate particulate and gaseous phases. Laboratory simulations will expose source-specific organic mixtures, including lignite leachates and synthetic standards, to controlled UV irradiation in an atmospheric simulation chamber. Analysis will employ GC×GC-TOFMS and specific compound isotopic analysis (CSIA) to track degradation products and changes in diagnostic ratios. Gas-particle partitioning coefficients will be modeled using polyparameter linear free energy relationships (pp-LFERs) based on measured physicochemical properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first kinetic framework for the photochemical aging of a suite of coal-derived organic tracers under realistic atmospheric conditions. It will yield refined parameterizations for gas-particle partitioning of these compounds, directly improving regional and global transport models. The results will critically evaluate the robustness of using specific organic compounds as inert markers for source apportionment in downwind environments.
proposals.novelty: While Simoneit's prior work identified sources and concentrations, this proposal uniquely integrates field transect data with controlled photochemical chamber experiments to deconvolute transformation pathways. Applying CSIA and pp-LFER modeling to this compound suite for atmospheric fate studies is a novel advancement beyond standard concentration-based tracer techniques.

proposals.title: Resolving Complex Emission Sources in Anthropogenically Impacted Environments: A Multivariate Factor Analysis of Organic and Inorganic Tracers
proposals.motivation: Complex emission sources, such as thermally affected waste dumps and industrial/urban areas, release intricate mixtures of organic compounds and inorganic species, confounding traditional source apportionment. Pre-2026, studies often analyze compound classes in isolation, missing the latent covariance structure that reveals true source processes. A holistic, multivariate approach is needed to disentangle these overlapping signatures for accurate environmental forensics and risk assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Positive Matrix Factorization (PMF) applied to a combined dataset of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), organic biomarkers (e.g., hopanes, steranes), and inorganic tracers (e.g., Hg species, S isotopes) will resolve distinct, interpretable source factors (e.g., coal pyrolysis, petroleum cracking, biogenic combustion, in-situ diagenesis) that are obscured in univariate analyses.
proposals.method: We will compile extensive pre-2026 ambient data (air particulates, sediments) from impacted sites, including coal mining regions and coastal industrial zones. The analytical suite will include GC-MS for PAHs and biomarkers, and ICP-MS/mercury analyzers for inorganic species. The core multivariate analysis will employ the US EPA PMF 5.0 model, using uncertainty estimates for each datum to weight the solution. Factor profiles will be validated against known source signatures (e.g., lignite resin markers, specific HgS/MeHg ratios) and interpreted using rotational diagnostics and bootstrap error estimation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified framework for source apportionment that integrates organic geochemistry and inorganic environmental chemistry. It will deliver quantitative emission profiles for complex, mixed anthropogenic sources, directly informing mitigation strategies. The methodology will advance the field beyond single-tracer or single-matrix studies, offering a more robust tool for environmental forensics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the concurrent application of PMF to a combined organic-inorganic tracer dataset from diverse environmental matrices, specifically targeting the covariance between classic petroleum biomarkers, PAH isomers, and toxic metal(loid) species—a synthesis not commonly attempted in pre-2026 source apportionment literature.

proposals.title: Constraining Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbon Emission Inventories in Southeast Asia via Inverse Modeling of Sedimentary Molecular Tracers
proposals.motivation: Accurate emission inventories for persistent organic pollutants like Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs) are critical for regional environmental management but are often uncertain. Bottom-up inventories rely on activity data and emission factors that may not reflect real-world combustion conditions and source mixtures. This research addresses this gap by using direct environmental measurements to validate and refine these inventories, a crucial step for effective policy.
proposals.hypothesis: An inverse modeling framework, using ambient concentration measurements of PAHs and specific molecular markers (tracers) in marine sediments, can quantitatively constrain and reduce the uncertainty in regional emission source apportionment for Southeast Asia.
proposals.method: We will analyze a network of short sediment cores from the South China Sea, quantifying PAH concentrations and diagnostic source tracers (e.g., retene for biomass, coronene for traffic). These data will serve as observational constraints for a Bayesian inverse modeling system. The system will use a regional atmospheric chemistry transport model (e.g., WRF-Chem) to simulate forward dispersion and deposition. The inversion will employ a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithm to optimize the posterior probability distribution of prior emission estimates from existing inventories (e.g., EDGAR), thereby providing refined, data-constrained emission fields.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, high-resolution emission map for anthropogenic PAHs in the Southeast Asian region. It will directly quantify the uncertainty reduction achieved by incorporating sedimentary tracer data. The methodology establishes a novel framework for using paleo-environmental archives to benchmark emission inventories for persistent organic pollutants.
proposals.novelty: While inverse modeling is used for greenhouse gases, its application to constrain PAH emissions using sedimentary molecular tracers and a full source-typing framework is novel. This approach uniquely leverages the integrative, long-term record provided by marine sediments, moving beyond short-term atmospheric monitoring to validate multi-year emission trends.

proposals.title: Comprehensive Non-Targeted Metabolomic Profiling of Mutagenic Smoke Extracts from Anthropogenic Combustion Sources Using Ultra-High-Resolution Mass Spectrometry
proposals.motivation: Mutagenic extracts from smoke (e.g., from coal waste dumps, biomass burning, or fossil fuel combustion) are complex mixtures where known toxicants like PAHs and Hg species represent only a fraction of the total organic burden. Pre-2026 environmental forensics, including Dr. Simoneit's work, often uses targeted tracer analysis, which may miss critical unidentified mutagenic compounds. A comprehensive molecular inventory is essential for accurate risk assessment of these pervasive pollution sources.
proposals.hypothesis: High-resolution, non-targeted metabolomic analysis of mutagenic smoke extracts will reveal a vast array of previously uncharacterized organic compounds, including novel heteroatom-containing species and high-molecular-weight derivatives, whose molecular signatures can be linked to specific combustion conditions and source materials.
proposals.method: Smoke condensates from controlled combustion of lignite, coal waste, and resins (reflecting Simoneit's expertise) will be extracted. Organic compounds will be analyzed using liquid chromatography coupled to an Orbitrap Elite or similar ultra-high-resolution mass spectrometer (resolving power >240,000 at m/z 200). Data will be processed using non-targeted workflows in software like MZmine 2, with molecular formula assignment (mass error < 2 ppm) and tentative identification via spectral matching against pre-2026 databases (e.g., NIST, MassBank). Multivariate statistical analysis (PCA, PLS-DA) will correlate chemical profiles with mutagenicity data (Ames test) and source type.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first high-definition molecular cartography of mutagenic smoke, moving beyond targeted tracer lists. It will identify novel candidate toxicants for future toxicological study and provide a robust chemical basis for refining source apportionment models in environmental forensics, linking specific molecular families to combustion processes.
proposals.novelty: While non-targeted metabolomics is established in biomedical fields, its rigorous application to complex environmental smoke extracts, particularly from anthropogenic sources like coal waste dumps, is novel. This approach transcends the standard PAH/tracer paradigm to discover new classes of combustion-derived compounds with potential health impacts.

proposals.title: Refined Source Apportionment of Urban Particulate Organic Carbon Using Health-Relevant Tracers: A Focus on Diesel and Gasoline Vehicle Emissions
proposals.motivation: Urban particulate matter (PM) contains complex organic carbon (OC) mixtures from diverse sources, with motor vehicle emissions being a major contributor to adverse health outcomes. Traditional source apportionment models often rely on bulk OC or non-specific tracers, failing to resolve the specific toxic components within the vehicular emission profile. This limits the ability of regulators to target the most harmful emission subsets for mitigation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating quantitative measurements of health-relevant organic compounds—specifically hopanes, steranes, and high-molecular-weight polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs)—as refined tracers in multivariate receptor models will significantly improve the resolution and accuracy of apportioning OC to diesel versus gasoline exhaust sources in urban aerosols.
proposals.method: We will collect PM2.5 samples from an urban traffic corridor. Organic compounds will be extracted and analyzed via gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) for target tracers, building on the PI's expertise in complex organic mixtures. Source apportionment will be performed using Positive Matrix Factorization (PMF) and Chemical Mass Balance (CMB) models. The key innovation is the creation of distinct, health-relevant chemical profiles for diesel and gasoline emissions by weighting the model input with the measured concentrations of the specific toxic tracers, rather than using total OC or elemental carbon alone.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a refined source apportionment methodology that explicitly links emission sources to their health-relevant chemical signatures. It will provide a more accurate quantification of the contribution of diesel and gasoline exhaust to ambient PM toxicity. The results will offer actionable data for air quality management aimed at reducing the most harmful vehicular emissions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate selection and use of known toxic organic compounds (e.g., specific PAHs, biomarkers) as the primary driver variables in receptor modeling for vehicular sources, moving beyond generic carbon fractions. This directly connects source apportionment outcomes with human health risk assessment, a linkage often inferred indirectly in pre-2026 studies.

proposals.title: Source Apportionment of Health-Relevant Organic Pollutants in Urban and Industrial Particulate Matter Using Chemical Fingerprinting and Multivariate Receptor Modeling
proposals.motivation: Urban and industrial particulate matter (PM) contains complex mixtures of toxic organic compounds, including polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), organometallics, and other hazardous species, which pose significant public health risks. Pre-2026 environmental forensics has successfully used chemical fingerprinting to distinguish pollution sources in sediments and geological systems, but its application to apportioning health-relevant organic species in ambient PM for direct human exposure assessment remains less developed. This gap hinders targeted mitigation strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Multivariate receptor modeling of detailed organic compound fingerprints (including PAHs, biomarkers, and specific organometallic tracers) in PM samples can quantitatively distinguish and apportion contributions from major health-relevant emission sources, such as coal combustion, vehicular traffic, and industrial processes, with greater specificity than traditional bulk analysis.
proposals.method: We will collect PM samples from strategic urban and industrial sites. Organic compounds will be extracted and analyzed via GC-MS and ICP-MS for detailed molecular fingerprints, including PAHs, hopanes, steranes, and specific organometallics (e.g., methylmercury species). Source apportionment will be performed using Positive Matrix Factorization (PMF) and Chemical Mass Balance (CMB) modeling, leveraging pre-2026 algorithms (e.g., US EPA PMF 5.0). Diagnostic ratios and multivariate statistics (PCA) will validate source profiles derived from local emitters like lignite combustion, traffic resins, and industrial waste.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for linking specific emission sources to the ambient concentrations of the most toxic organic PM components, directly informing regulatory health risk assessments. It will advance environmental forensics by adapting geological and sediment fingerprinting techniques to the dynamic atmospheric compartment, creating a transferable methodology for urban air quality management.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of geological biomarker tracers (e.g., from lignite studies) and organometallic species (e.g., MeHg) with classic PAH fingerprints in multivariate PM models for urban air represents a significant, non-obvious cross-disciplinary application of forensic geochemistry to public health-oriented aerosol science.

proposals.title: Dual Carbon and Hydrogen Isotope Fingerprinting of Plant Wax Lipids as Tracers for Anthropogenic and Natural Organic Matter in Urban-Coastal Sediments
proposals.motivation: Urban-coastal environments are critical sinks for both natural terrestrial organic matter and anthropogenic pollutants, including polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) from fossil fuel combustion and waste. Pre-2026 studies, including Simoneit's work on PAH sources and coal waste, highlight the need for robust tracers to disentangle these complex organic inputs. Compound-specific stable isotope analysis (CSIA) of plant waxes (e.g., n-alkanes, n-alkanoic acids) offers a powerful but underexploited tool for this purpose, as their isotopic signatures (δ13C, δ2H) reflect vegetation type and hydrological conditions, providing a baseline against which anthropogenic signals can be identified.
proposals.hypothesis: The δ13C and δ2H isotopic composition of vascular plant wax lipids in short-core sediments from an urbanized coastal system will reveal distinct end-members for natural C3/C4 vegetation and will show significant isotopic shifts in layers contaminated with anthropogenic hydrocarbons, enabling the quantitative apportionment of natural versus anthropogenic organic carbon sources.
proposals.method: Sediment cores will be collected from the Terengganu coastal area, Malaysia, a region with known PAH inputs. Total lipid extracts will be obtained via accelerated solvent extraction. Target wax compounds (C25-C33 n-alkanes, C22-C30 n-alkanoic acids) will be isolated using silica gel chromatography and urea adduction. Compound-specific δ13C and δ2H analysis will be performed using Gas Chromatography-Isotope Ratio Mass Spectrometry (GC-IRMS). Isotopic data will be integrated with PAH concentration profiles and subjected to multivariate statistical analysis (Principal Component Analysis, Hierarchical Cluster Analysis) to correlate isotopic shifts with anthropogenic event layers.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, multi-isotope framework for using plant waxes not just as paleovegetation proxies, but as active tracers for identifying and quantifying anthropogenic perturbations in the organic carbon cycle. It will directly contribute to refining source apportionment models in contaminated coastal sediments, bridging Simoneit's expertise in organic tracers and pollution studies. The resulting isotopic end-member library will be applicable to other urban-impacted deltaic systems worldwide.
proposals.novelty: While plant wax δ13C and δ2H are established in paleoclimatology, their combined application as direct tracers to differentiate natural and anthropogenic organic matter in modern polluted coastal sediments is novel. This approach innovatively repackages established geochemical isotope techniques to solve a pressing environmental forensics problem, moving beyond concentration-based or single-compound tracer methods.

proposals.title: Atmospheric Oxidation Pathways and Sink Mechanisms for Terrestrial Plant Wax Lipids: A Laboratory and Field Tracer Study
proposals.motivation: Plant wax lipids (e.g., n-alkanes, n-alkanoic acids) are major components of terrestrial organic aerosol and serve as key paleoenvironmental proxies in sediments. However, their chemical transformations and lifetimes upon emission into the troposphere are poorly constrained, creating a critical gap between source emission signals and the preserved geological record. Quantifying these atmospheric processing pathways is essential for accurately interpreting biomarker records and understanding the organic carbon cycle.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that hydroxyl radical (OH)-initiated oxidation is the dominant sink for plant wax n-alkanes in the troposphere, leading to functionalized products (alkanols, alkanoic acids) and fragmentation compounds, with reaction rates and product distributions predictable based on carbon chain length and molecular structure.
proposals.method: We will employ a dual laboratory and field approach. Laboratory smog chamber experiments will expose pure C25-C35 n-alkane standards to controlled OH radicals, with products analyzed via Thermal Desorption–Two-Dimensional Gas Chromatography–Time-of-Flight Mass Spectrometry (TD-GC×GC-TOFMS). Specific oxidation products will be identified as molecular tracers. Concurrently, we will collect high-volume aerosol samples from a coastal site (e.g., Terengganu, Malaysia) to capture both fresh and aged continental air masses. Samples will be analyzed for the parent wax compounds and the laboratory-identified oxidation tracers using GC-MS, with multivariate statistical analysis (PCA, PMF) applied to apportion sources and degradation states.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish, for the first time, a quantitative framework for the atmospheric degradation of high-molecular-weight plant waxes. It will identify specific molecular tracers for atmospheric processing, directly linking aerosol chemistry to sedimentary biomarker interpretation. The results will refine models of organic aerosol aging and the fidelity of lipid-based paleoclimate proxies.
proposals.novelty: While Simoneit's prior work identifies sources and in-situ thermal alteration of biomarkers, this study proactively investigates their pre-depositional atmospheric chemical fate—a largely overlooked but critical control on the biomarker record. The combination of controlled oxidation experiments with targeted field tracer analysis using advanced GC×GC represents a novel, mechanistic approach to this problem.

ideas.title: LPG as a 'Trojan Horse' Pollutant: Applying Pharmaceutical Metabolism Models to Secondary Ozone Formation
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption that LPG is a cleaner fuel by investigating its high contribution to Ozone Formation Potential (OFP) as indicative of significant secondary pollution. Drawing an analogy from pharmacology/toxicology, where a benign 'prodrug' metabolizes into a toxic agent, we treat LPG as an atmospheric precursor. The analogous solution—mapping metabolic pathways to identify and quantify reactive intermediates—is adapted by applying detailed chemical mechanism models (like MCM) to map the full atmospheric oxidation pathways of LPG. The research would systematically trace the secondary formation of carbonyl compounds (e.g., acetaldehyde, formaldehyde) and peroxy radicals from LPG oxidation, quantifying their relative contribution to OFP under real-world urban photochemical regimes. This shifts the assessment from primary emissions to a full atmospheric 'chemical lifecycle' analysis, potentially revealing LPG's role as a significant indirect pollutant source despite its cleaner combustion profile.

ideas.title: Using a Tracer Compound as a Universal Scaling Factor, Validated by Multi-Class Chemical Fingerprinting
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that scaling a VOC inventory by burn-specific toluene alone can accurately represent all key SOA precursor classes for every fuel. It draws an analogy from environmental forensics, where complex hydrocarbon sources (like crude oil) are apportioned using conserved biomarker tracers (e.g., hopanes) after initial, detailed fingerprinting establishes source-specific ratios to other compound classes. The adapted research solution is a two-phase study. First, conduct controlled combustion experiments to create a comprehensive database mapping the emission ratios of major VOC classes (aromatics, alkanes, alkenes, oxygenates) to toluene for a wide range of fuels. This establishes the 'fuel-specific fingerprint.' Second, design a field campaign to test the predictive power of this method: identify the fuel source, measure toluene, apply the corresponding fingerprint ratios from the database to predict emissions of other VOCs, and compare these predictions to direct measurements of the full VOC suite. This tests the core assumption's validity and defines its constraints across fuel types.

ideas.title: Drone-enabled dynamic validation of LPG-influenced ozone chemistry in urban plumes
ideas.description: This project integrates drone-based mass spectrometry with urban air quality modeling by deploying UAVs to sample fresh and aged urban plumes downwind of LPG source areas. The high-resolution vertical and horizontal gradients of LPG tracers and ozone precursors measured in real-time are used to directly test and refine the photochemical model's representation of LPG chemistry and dispersion. This enables a shift from using generic, inventory-based emission factors to deriving dynamic, observationally-constrained factors that evolve with atmospheric processing. The result is a fundamentally improved, spatially-explicit simulation of ozone formation tied to actual plume chemistry, enhancing predictive accuracy for pollution mitigation strategies.

ideas.title: Real-Time Evolution of Source-Resolved VOC Reactivity in Oxidation Chambers
ideas.description: This research integrates fast, online process gas chromatography with dynamic, time-resolved source profiles to monitor VOC oxidation in chambers. Instead of using static fingerprints, it employs real-world, evolving emission data (e.g., from gasoline fleets with varying technology shares) to create reactive gas mixtures. This allows for the direct experimental quantification of how changes in source composition—driven by technology turnover—affect real-time oxidation rates, radical budgets, and secondary product yields. The result is a predictive experimental platform that can validate and constrain models of future air quality impacts based on projected emission evolution.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: derive fuel type from aged aerosol using forensic proteomics techniques
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from forensic proteomics and paleoproteomics to solve the inverse problem of deriving biomass fuel type from aged atmospheric aerosols. In the analogous field, the original biological source of severely degraded organic material is determined by identifying surviving, source-specific peptide markers via mass spectrometry and using pattern recognition algorithms for classification. The adapted idea applies this framework to atmospheric chemistry: we will develop analytical methods to detect persistent, fuel-characteristic molecular fragments within the complex, oxidized chemical signature of aged particles. By constructing a reference library from controlled burning and aging experiments for major fuel types, we can train a machine learning model to perform the inverse classification, scaling the method from laboratory validation to application on field-collected, aged aerosol samples.

ideas.title: High-Throughput Robotic Chamber for LPG VOC Mixture Toxicity Screening
ideas.description: This research integrates automated synthesis robotics with environmental chamber technology to conduct parallel, high-throughput experiments. The system will automatically prepare and inject a wide array of real-world LPG fuel samples and synthetic mixtures into multiple small-scale chambers for controlled photochemical aging. The resulting oxidized mixtures, dominated by propane and butane tracers but containing their full complex secondary chemistry, will be directly transferred in a high-throughput format to in vitro toxicological assays. This approach allows for the rapid, systematic mapping of how variations in LPG source composition influence the formation and health impacts of secondary organic aerosols and other oxidation products.

ideas.title: Dynamic Source Apportionment Using Time-Varying Chemical Fingerprints
ideas.description: This idea challenges the static source profile assumption in air pollution source apportionment by drawing an analogy from paleoclimatology. In ice core science, the chemical fingerprint of dust sources (like deserts) is understood to change over geological time due to climate-driven alterations in vegetation, weathering, and atmospheric circulation. Scientists construct evolving source signatures by correlating chemical layers in ice cores with known historical climate events. Adapted to atmospheric chemistry, this approach involves building a temporally evolving library of vehicle emission profiles (VOC, NOx, PM) that tracks changes due to fleet turnover (e.g., phase-out of older diesel, hybrid penetration) and engine technology advances (e.g., gasoline direct injection, after-treatment systems). The research would develop a framework to integrate these dynamic profiles into receptor modeling, allowing for more accurate attribution of urban pollution sources over multi-year periods, treating source signatures as variables that 'drift' with technological time rather than fixed constants.

ideas.title: Dual: Quantifying Net Organic Aerosol via a Mass-Balance Accounting Framework
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of dual-entry accounting from corporate finance to solve the problem of quantifying competing processes in organic aerosol evolution. In finance, net profit is not measured directly but derived from the sum of all revenues and expenses, with the system constrained by a balance sheet identity. Similarly, we propose to treat the atmospheric OA mass as a balance sheet, where the net observed change is not a direct measurement of either process alone but the sum of SOA production (a mass inflow) and POA volatilization loss (a mass outflow). The adapted research idea is to develop a mass-balance accounting framework or analysis method that uses this constraint. By simultaneously quantifying these flows—potentially using tracer species, volatility distributions, or model ensembles—the framework would explicitly solve for the 'expense' of POA loss, thereby directly quantifying its role in suppressing the net SOA accumulation observed in the atmosphere.

ideas.title: Mechanistic quantification of tracer ion transformation pathways under controlled atmospheric oxidation
ideas.description: This research moves beyond field observation to experimentally determine the chemical stability of the POA tracer ion. By exposing the pure tracer compound and tracer-doped particles to controlled oxidants (OH, O3, NO3) in a laboratory chamber, we can directly track its molecular transformation using advanced mass spectrometry. This approach will identify specific decomposition products and reaction pathways, enabling the quantification of degradation rate constants. The results will provide a mechanistic, kinetic basis for assessing the tracer's robustness across the full range of atmospheric oxidation conditions.

ideas.title: Constraining regional SOA model biases with ambient tracer ion validation
ideas.description: This research fuses ambient validation and model integration by using the POA tracer ion method as an observational benchmark for evaluating fuel-specific SOA parameterizations within regional atmospheric models. By deploying the method in diverse real-world plumes, it quantifies the bias between modeled and observed SOA production for specific fuel sources. These bias metrics are then used to develop and apply empirical correction factors to the underlying chamber-derived parameterizations before their integration into the model. This creates a novel, observationally-constrained scaling pathway that reduces model uncertainty and improves the climate-relevant prediction of SOA from anthropogenic and biomass burning emissions.

ideas.title: Bayesian Probabilistic Apportionment of LPG's Secondary Ozone Formation Potential
ideas.description: This research integrates Bayesian source apportionment of ambient VOC measurements with detailed chemical mechanism modeling to probabilistically quantify the contribution of Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG) to secondary ozone formation. The approach explicitly quantifies the uncertainty in LPG's source contribution and propagates it through OFP calculations, testing the hypothesis that LPG is a dominant, uncertain driver of secondary pollution despite its 'cleaner' primary emission profile. This creates a robust framework to evaluate the net atmospheric benefit of LPG adoption policies by comparing the probability distributions of its OFP against other sources, providing decision-makers with a risk-aware assessment of fuel-switching strategies.

ideas.title: Causal Attribution of Seasonal VOC Composition Dynamics: Disentangling Meteorological, Biogenic, and Anthropogenic Drivers
ideas.description: This research moves beyond testing for seasonal variation to explicitly identify the causal mechanisms behind it. It implements a continuous, high-resolution monitoring campaign for VOC speciation alongside a comprehensive suite of co-located meteorological, traffic, and boundary layer height data. By applying causal inference and timeseries analysis methods, the study will quantify the relative contribution and interaction of key drivers—such as temperature-dependent biogenic emissions, photochemical aging, and cold-start vehicle emissions—to observed compositional changes. This approach transforms a descriptive correlation study into a predictive, mechanistic understanding of atmospheric VOC dynamics.

ideas.title: Common-Atmosphere Robotic Sequential Sampling for High-Throughput, Comparable Fuel Reactivity Studies
ideas.description: This system overcomes the fatal flaw of parallel chamber variability by creating a single, master environmental chamber that provides a perfectly uniform and highly controlled reactive atmosphere (e.g., consistent OH radical concentration, UV flux). An array of individual, miniature sample vessels containing different fuels is placed inside this common environment. A high-precision robotic sampler, equipped with rapid analytical instrumentation, sequentially and repeatedly extracts and analyzes the headspace gas from each vessel over time. This approach guarantees that all fuel samples react under identical conditions, enabling direct, high-throughput comparison of reaction rates and secondary organic aerosol formation potentials with unprecedented experimental consistency.

ideas.title: Mechanistic interactome of multi-precursor chemistry dictates fuel-specific SOA yield
ideas.description: This research establishes that the SOA-forming potential of a fuel type is governed not by a dominant precursor class, but by the nonlinear chemical interactions within its unique precursor mixture. It identifies and quantifies key interactive processes—such as OH recycling, cross-peroxy radical reactions, and shared oxidation product pathways—that either synergistically enhance or inhibit aerosol mass yield. The outcome is a parameterizable framework that predicts SOA yield from a fuel's detailed precursor spectrum, moving beyond descriptive classification to mechanistic prediction for atmospheric models.

ideas.title: Apply Blind Source Separation from Neuroimaging to Deconvolve AMS Spectra
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from neuroimaging to the aerosol mass spectrometry (AMS) deconvolution problem. In fMRI, Blind Source Separation methods like Independent Component Analysis (ICA) are used to disentangle overlapping neural signals from noise and artifacts without requiring prior templates. The analogous problem in AMS is separating mixed primary (POA) and secondary (SOA) organic aerosol signatures in complex mass spectral datasets. By treating the time series of mass spectra as a mixture of independent source contributions, spatial-temporal ICA can be applied to decompose the data into statistically independent components. Each component would represent a distinct source or process (e.g., traffic POA, cooking POA, highly oxidized SOA) with its characteristic mass spectrum and temporal profile, enabling a more robust, model-agnostic separation than traditional factor analysis methods like PMF.

ideas.title: Precursor Co-emission Networks: A Systems Biology Approach to SOA Formation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a single dominant precursor class for a given fuel type by drawing an analogy from systems biology. In metabolic networks, pathway output is an emergent property of interacting compounds, not a single dominant enzyme. The analogous solution involves mapping entire interaction networks to identify critical nodes and synergies. Adapted to atmospheric chemistry, this proposes moving beyond neat classifications to model SOA formation from a given fuel (e.g., pine emissions, diesel exhaust) as a function of the co-emission network. The research would experimentally and computationally map how interactive oxidation within the complex mixture of VOCs—including minor species—modulates oxidation pathways, radical budgets, and particle-phase reactions, ultimately controlling SOA yield and composition in a non-linear, non-additive manner.

ideas.title: Dynamic Predictive Framework for Non-Stationary VOC Source Fingerprints from Evolving Mobile Sources
ideas.description: This research develops a predictive, mechanistic model to forecast the evolution of VOC source profiles from mobile sources, moving beyond descriptive updates. The system integrates a high-resolution fleet turnover model with technology-specific emission modules, linking parameters like after-treatment system adoption and fuel standards to changes in VOC speciation. This dynamic framework is continuously constrained and validated by targeted ambient measurements and near-source testing, enabling both retrospective analysis and projection of future source fingerprints for robust, forward-looking air quality management and source apportionment.

ideas.title: Parameterization Transfer via Emulator Networks
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational neuroscience, where detailed but expensive models are used to train simplified emulators for system-level integration. The analogous problem of integrating high-fidelity, fuel-specific secondary organic aerosol (SOA) parameterizations from chamber studies into regional climate models is solved by creating machine learning-based emulator networks. These emulators are trained to replicate the complex, non-linear SOA formation yields and properties predicted by detailed process models or observed in chamber data for specific fuel types and conditions. The resulting lightweight parameterizations can then be embedded within the atmospheric chemistry modules of models like WRF-Chem or CMAQ, enabling regionally specific, fuel-aware SOA predictions in climate simulations without prohibitive computational overhead.

ideas.title: Validating Smog-Chamber Dilution via Tracer Release and Multi-Scale Modeling
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that smog-chamber aging fully represents real plume dilution by adapting a validation method from hydrology. In groundwater studies, field tracer tests are used to ground-truth lab column experiments. Adapted here, the idea is to perform a coordinated dual experiment: 1) a controlled, instrumented field release of smoke with an inert tracer to measure real atmospheric dilution dynamics, and 2) a parallel chamber experiment replicating the initial conditions. By comparing the temporal evolution of the tracer and smoke components between the 3D field plume and the 0D chamber, the research will directly test and quantify the fidelity of chamber dilution, potentially leading to correction factors or improved chamber protocols to better mimic atmospheric mixing.

ideas.title: Hybrid CIMS-GC system for real-time VOC oxidation kinetics and product validation
ideas.description: This redesigned approach combines a chemical ionization mass spectrometer (CIMS) for fast, real-time monitoring of radical intermediates and oxygenated products with a parallel process gas chromatograph for detailed speciation. The CIMS provides the necessary millisecond-to-second temporal resolution to capture rapid oxidation kinetics, while the GC delivers periodic, validated quantification of specific isomers and stable products that are challenging for soft ionization mass spectrometry alone. Data from both instruments are fused using custom software to create a comprehensive, time-aligned picture of the reaction mechanism. This system overcomes the fatal speed limitation of GC-only methods, enabling true real-time kinetic studies of VOC oxidation with robust molecular identification.

ideas.title: Apply Bayesian Mixing Models from Ecology to VOC Source Apportionment
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to a parallel problem in ecology—determining animal diet proportions from stable isotope data—to the challenge of VOC source apportionment. In ecology, Bayesian mixing models (like MixSIAR) rigorously quantify the proportional contributions of multiple food sources by incorporating prior information and full uncertainty propagation. The adapted research idea applies this framework to VOC data: ambient VOC mixtures are analogous to the consumer's tissue, and source emission profiles are analogous to the food sources. The model will use Bayesian inference to estimate posterior probability distributions for source contributions, formally integrating prior knowledge (e.g., from emission inventories) and comprehensively accounting for uncertainties in both source profiles and ambient measurements, providing a more statistically rigorous alternative to traditional methods like PMF.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Monitoring Network Design to Urban Air Quality Sampling
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a single roadside location captures full urban transport emission variability by drawing an analogy from ecological monitoring. In ecology, representativeness is achieved through stratified sampling and sensor networks across environmental gradients (e.g., NEON). The adapted solution is to design an urban air quality monitoring network stratified by transport-relevant domains: major highways, arterial roads, local streets with different canyon effects, and urban background. By analyzing concurrent data from a network of low-cost sensors within this designed framework, the research can statistically quantify the spatial representativeness error of a single roadside site for key pollutants, directly informing robust policy design and siting guidelines for regulatory monitoring.

ideas.title: Apply Paleolimnological Sediment Core Analysis to VOC Trend Monitoring
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from paleolimnology, which uses dated sediment cores to reconstruct long-term environmental change, this research adapts the core archive concept to atmospheric monitoring. The analogous solution involves analyzing sequential layers in sediment as a temporal record. Adapted to the VOC monitoring problem, the idea is to deploy a network of passive air samplers with sorbents that accumulate VOCs over defined, consecutive periods. These samplers act as 'air cores,' creating a continuous, integrated, and high-resolution archive. Analyzing these sequential 'layers' post-LPG policy shift allows for the reconstruction of precise VOC speciation and concentration trends over time, filling gaps between traditional monitoring campaigns and providing a robust long-term impact assessment.

ideas.title: High-Throughput Robotic Chamber for Validating Plume Dilution Models
ideas.description: This research program develops a robotic environmental chamber system to perform hundreds of parallel aging experiments on diverse smoke samples. The massive, consistent dataset of aerosol evolution under controlled dilution conditions is then used to systematically challenge and validate high-fidelity atmospheric models of real plume dispersion and chemistry. This direct, iterative feedback between high-throughput empirical data and computational models allows for the precise identification of when and why chamber conditions diverge from atmospheric reality. The ultimate goal is to create a validated scaling framework that translates chamber results into accurate predictions of smoke aging in the complex, turbulent atmosphere.

ideas.title: Attributing VOC trends to LPG policy using counterfactual modeling and observational constraints
ideas.description: This study moves beyond passive monitoring to actively attribute changes in VOC composition to the LPG policy shift. It combines a sustained, high-frequency VOC measurement network with a detailed emissions inventory and a regional atmospheric chemistry model. By constructing and comparing modeled scenarios with and without the policy intervention, constrained by real-world observations, the research quantifies the policy's specific impact while controlling for confounding variables. This approach transforms correlation into robust causal attribution, providing defensible evidence for policy effectiveness.

ideas.title: Adapting Pharmaceutical High-Throughput Screening Robotics for Atmospheric Chamber Experiments
ideas.description: This idea adapts the proven high-throughput screening (HTS) methodology from pharmaceutical drug discovery to atmospheric chemistry. In pharma, automated robotic systems test thousands of chemical compounds in parallel using microplates, precise liquid handling, controlled incubation, and inline detection. The adapted solution applies this architecture to smog chamber experiments. A robotic platform would be developed to handle a specialized 'atmospheric reaction plate' containing many isolated, small-volume chambers. The system would automatically dose each chamber with a different fuel or VOC sample, expose all chambers to identical, controlled atmospheric conditions (light, oxidants, humidity), and use integrated analytical probes (e.g., miniature FTIR, PTR-MS) to monitor chemical evolution in real-time and in parallel. This would transform atmospheric reactivity testing from a sequential, sample-limited process into a high-throughput, massively parallel assay, enabling rapid screening of fuel formulations, emission sources, or chemical mechanisms.

ideas.title: A Hybrid Non-linear Transformation and Physically-Constrained Multivariate Model for Robust AMS Spectral Deconvolution
ideas.description: This approach directly addresses the critical non-linearity in AMS spectra by developing a two-stage hybrid model. First, a neural network-based model is trained on laboratory mixtures to learn a non-linear mapping that corrects for matrix and instrumental effects, creating a more linear latent space. Second, traditional chemometric factor resolution is performed in this conditioned space, with tight integration of laboratory reference spectra and their uncertainties as hard constraints to guide the solution. This results in a more physically realistic and stable separation of POA and SOA factors, significantly reducing rotational ambiguity and producing more reliable source apportionment for complex ambient datasets.

ideas.title: Bayesian Multi-Model Inference with Diagnostic Checks for Robust VOC Source Apportionment
ideas.description: This approach implements a rigorous Bayesian source apportionment framework for VOCs that is explicitly robust to model misspecification. It employs Bayesian model averaging across a carefully designed ensemble of candidate models with different source profiles, numbers, and error assumptions to integrate structural uncertainty into the final estimates. Mandatory prior and posterior predictive checks are used to diagnostically validate model assumptions against the observed data. The final output provides combined source contributions with uncertainty intervals that are credible and account for both parametric uncertainty and fundamental uncertainty in the correct model form.

ideas.title: Adaptive Drone Swarm for 3D Plume Tomography
ideas.description: This idea adapts the swarm-based, adaptive sampling strategies used in oceanography for mapping dynamic underwater plumes to the atmospheric sciences. The analogous solution from marine biology involves using coordinated swarms of autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) that communicate to adaptively map oceanographic features. Adapted to this context, a swarm of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), each equipped with miniaturized mass spectrometers, would be deployed. The swarm uses networked adaptive algorithms to autonomously reconfigure its 3D formation in real-time based on initial plume readings, dynamically concentrating sampling effort on chemical gradients and plume edges. This transforms the system from a single mobile sampler into an intelligent, distributed sensor network capable of constructing high-resolution, real-time 3D tomographic maps of plume composition and aging processes.
